You may only target heroes with Apocalypse Automaton. You cannot target anything other than heroes.
If an effect requires you to target something other than a hero (e.g. Arc Light Sentinel), Apocalypse Automaton ignores that requirement because it cannot target anything other than a hero.
If an effect restricts you from targeting a hero (e.g. an effect that says “hero can not be targeted this turn.”), Apocalypse Automaton can not target that hero.
You may choose to target 0 opposing heroes. If you do, the combat chain closes because there are no valid attack targets, and Apocalypse Automaton is cleared and goes to the graveyard.
Damage for each target hero is calculated separately, based on the defending cards that have been declared for that hero.

If you attack 2 or more heroes using Apocalypse Automaton, during the Defend step each hero in clockwise order declares the defending cards for themselves, starting with the player left of the attacker. Once a player has declared defending cards for themselves, players in clockwise order may add defending cards (e.g. with Protect), and then you move onto the next player declaring defending cards for themselves etc.

If an effect would trigger for Apocalypse Automaton dealing damage or hitting a hero, it triggers for each hero it deals damage to or hits. E.g. If you play Pummel on Apocalypse Automaton, each hero hit would discard a card.
Removing a steam counter is optional. If you choose not to remove a steam counter, it is simply destroyed.

If you play a Mechanologist attack action card and then put Autosave Script into the arena before the damage step of combat, the card will trigger on-hit. If you put Autosave Script into the arena after the damage step of combat, the card will not retroactively trigger, even if it had hit.

Players draw up to their intellect at the end of their turn. If you play this on your turn, you will typically draw one less card than usual. Then at the end of your opponent’s turn, they’d typically draw one less card. Then this would be destroyed at the start of your turn.

Looking at the top of your deck is a static ability that affects the game rules, allowing you to look at the top of your deck as though it were standard private information to you. You don’t need to have priority to look at the top of your deck.

When any player would look at, or reveal cards from the top of your deck, those cards are still considered to be in the same order on the top of your deck, until they are explicitly moved somewhere else. E.g. If you opt 3, while looking at the top 3 cards of your deck, it does not change what card is on the top of your deck; you can not look at the 4th card as though it were on top of your deck unless you then put those 3 cards on the bottom as part of the opt ability.

You can only attempt to play a Mechanologist item with cost 0 or 1 from the top of your deck if you are able to look at it first. If another effect prevents you from looking at that card, you can not play it.
While you may look at the top of your deck at any time, you may only play that card when you have priority.
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.

If you play the top card of your deck, you may not look at the next top card of your deck until you have finished playing the card. I.e. you cannot use information about the next card to determine how you want to play the card.

If you resolve an effect that changes the top card of your deck, you may not look at the next top card of your deck until you have finished resolving the entire layer of the effect. I.e. you cannot use information about the next card to determine how to resolve the remainder of the effects.

If you play a Mechanologist attack action card and then put MHz Script into the arena before the Link step of combat, the card will get go again and will generate an action point for you. The combat steps go: Layer step (when you play), Attack step (when you attack), Defend step (when they defend), Reaction step (either can play reactions), Damage step (calculate and apply damage), Resolution step (resolve triggered effects), Link step (gain action point from go again, play another attack).
You choose the value of X when you play this card. You may choose X=0. If you do this card has no effect (but you still get an action point from go again).

You do not have to have hit on each chain link in a row for this condition to be met.

Effects that put a -1{d} counter on an equipment may target and put the counter on Teklovossen, the Mechropotent.
If Teklovossen, the Mechropotent defends an attack with Phantasm, the Phantasm does not trigger because Teklovossen, the Mechropotent is not an attack action card.

If you increase the {p} of this to 13 or more before the damage step of combat, the card will trigger on-hit. If you increase the power after the damage step of combat, the card will not retroactively trigger, even if it had hit.
This on-hit only applies to aura tokens. If a card is not an aura or a token, it will not be destroyed by this effect.

The owner of the item is considered the player who started the game with that item, you are considered to be the controller while it is in the arena. When it is destroyed or banished, it goes to the owner’s graveyard or banished zone.
Items in the arena mean any item that is in any zone in the arena and not a sub-card of another permanent (i.e. not under another card).
You determine the value of X when you play this card.
The cost to play this is twice the value of X (e.g. X+X).
If X is 6 or greater, you create the Runechants before the target hero discards the cards. There is no priority between creating the Runechants and the discard effect.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.
Players choose a card from their graveyard and put it on the bottom of their deck clockwise around the table. The chosen card is known to all players.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.
Each other hero means all heroes except yourself.

If an item you control is destroyed before the link step of combat, the card will get go again and will generate an action point for you. The combat steps go: Layer step (when you play), Attack step (when you attack), Defend step (when they defend), Reaction step (either can play reactions), Damage step (calculate and apply damage), Resolution step (resolve triggered effects), Link step (gain action point from go again, play another attack).
This triggers when this becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, you choose whether to destroy an item you control.
You may choose an equipment, item, or weapon, even if it has 0 steam counters on it.
If any item or equipment is banished from paying the boost cost for this, this gets the +1{p}. The banished card does not have to be a Mechanologist card.
Cards that were banished from paying the boost cost of other cards do not count toward this effect.
If this enters or leaves the arena after the target equipment has been activated, it does not negate any of the existing activated abilities on the stack of that equipment.
If the controller of the equipment changes, the effect still lasts until the end of the original controller’s next turn.
When the activated ability resolves, If the equipment is already defending, it is not retroactively removed from the combat chain as a defending card.
The first ability is a triggered ability, players gain priority and can respond to it before it resolves
If this is controlled by a player other than its owner, the cost of its activated ability puts it on the bottom of its owner’s deck, not the controlling player’s deck.

You may put a steam counter on an item with crank while it is on the stack. If the item would enter the arena with steam counters, it gains that many steam counters plus the one you added by activating this.
If this is not defending, it does not get +X{d}.
This is a static ability. If the number of Evos you have equipped changes, it changes the value of X while this is defending.
If an item is destroyed after this has been played, it will retroactively get the +2{p}.
This triggers when this becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, you choose whether to destroy an item you control.
You can attack with this any number of times each turn, as long as you have the resources (i..e action points, and steam counters).
If two or more Mechanologist items enter the arena under your control simultaneously, this triggers for each of those items.

The number of steam counters is checked at the time the trigger resolves on the stack. If this has 5 steam counters and is triggered twice, it will only gain 1 steam counter after the triggers resolve.
If you gain control of a Mechanologist item that is already in the arena, this does not trigger.
If you are Dash or Dash, Inventor Extraordinaire, and you start the game by putting an item in the arena, this triggers and you may put a steam counter on this.
A Gun is not a Pistol.
You may target an equipment, item, or weapon, even if it has 0 steam counters on it.
You choose the value of X when you play this card. You may choose X=0. If you do, you do not banish nor return any cards.
If you control an item owned by another player, if you banish it, it will return under the control of its owner, not you.
Items that are banished and return to the arena are considered new cards. They lose any steam counters on them, and then return to the arena (and may have a new set of steam counters on them).
You choose the order that the cards are banished, then you choose the order that the cards return.
Token items that are banished cease to exist when they are banished and do not return to the arena.
You choose the value of X when you play this card. You may choose X=0. If you do this card has no effect (but you still get an action point from go again).
Each condition of “x or more” is evaluated independently. If you have 4 or more Evos equipped, all of the conditions are met and you will get all of the effects.

This is a static ability. If the number of Evos you have equipped changes, it changes what effects the card generates. E.g. if you go from 4 to 3 Evos equipped, this will no longer get +1{p}. If you go from 3 to 2 Evos equipped, this will no longer get go again.
A Gun is not a Pistol.
If the attack does not have overpower when this defends, but gains overpower after this card defends, this does not trigger and will not get the +2{d}.
You only draw a card if you played an Evo as an instant due to Teklovossen’s activated ability. You do not draw a card from activating the ability and not playing an Evo this way.

Once you play an Evo as an instant using this activated ability’s effect, the effect triggers. When the trigger resolves you draw a card. There is priority between playing the Evo as an instant and drawing the card.
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.
If you’ve transformed into Teklovossen, the Mechropotent, this is no longer your hero and no longer allows you to play Evos from your banished zone (or activate this hero’s ability).
Each condition of “x or more” is evaluated independently. If you have 4 or more Evos equipped, all of the conditions are met and you will get all of the effects.
This is a static ability. If the number of Evos you have equipped changes, it changes what effects the card generates. E.g. if you go from 4 to 3 Evos equipped, this will no longer get +3{p}.
This ability is not functional while this card is defending on the combat chain.
Removing a steam counter is optional. If you choose not to remove a steam counter, it is simply destroyed and you are dealt 1 damage.
You may choose 0, 1, or 2 items other than this to be destroyed.
X includes both this (Tick Tock Clock) and the other items that are destroyed. E.g. A maximum of 3 damage.
If you do not play the Crouching Tiger on your next turn, it remains in the banished zone for the rest of the game and can not be played in subsequent turns with this effect.
There is no priority between drawing the cards and pitching red cards.
Pitching two red cards is optional. Even if you have two red cards, you may choose to banish your hand instead.
If you choose to pitch 2 red cards, you must pitch exactly 2 red cards. You can not pitch 1 red card and then banish your hand, nor can you pitch additional cards as part of this pitch effect.
When you pitch cards this way, you may still trigger effects due to pitching (Dromai etc.) and you still gain the resources from pitching (e.g. you would gain 2 resources).
If this is already defended by two or more action cards, and then an item you control is destroyed, this gains overpower but the defending action cards are not retroactively removed.
This triggers when this becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, you choose whether to destroy an item you control.
When you play this card, you choose if you want to boost 0, 1, or 2 times (before you pay any costs). You cannot boost, then look at the top of your deck to decide if you want to boost a second time.
If you do not have any cards in your deck, you can not boost this card. If you do not have 2 cards in your deck, you can not boost this card twice.
If you banish a Mechanologist card with both boost costs, you do not gain two action points from go again. You may still only gain 1 action point from any amount of go again on a card.
Boosting twice will trigger effects that trigger whenever you boost.E.g. for the purposes of Hyper X-3.
Boosting twice will increase the total number of times you’ve boosted this combat chain/turn by 2. (e.g. for the purposes of Maximum Velocity).
Putting this card on the bottom of the deck does not close the combat chain.
When this card is removed from the chain link, Last Known Information is used to determine whether it has go again and if the attacker will gain an action point or not.
Vigor triggers and resolves during the start of turn phase. There is no priority and it can not be responded to.
Each condition of “x or more” is evaluated independently. If you have 4 or more Evos equipped, all of the conditions are met and you will get all of the effects.
This is a static ability. If the number of Evos you have equipped changes, it changes what effects the card generates. E.g. if you go from 4 to 3 Evos equipped, this will no longer get +3{p}.
This ability is not functional while this card is defending on the combat chain.
This has an optional additional cost. You do not have to charge your soul to play this.
Charging a yellow card in any way other than this card’s optional effect does not meet the condition of its second ability.
You may play Wax On and Wax Off on the same chain link. The condition of Wax Off is met, even if Wax On did not resolve as a layer on the stack before Wax Off.
What’s next, Zero to Forty?

Aggressive Pounce’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you haven’t intimidated an opponent yet this turn you when you attack with this and then you do, this will get go again.

Go again is checked during the resolution step. If you haven’t intimidated an opponent yet this turn you when you attack with this and then you do before the attack’s resolution step (i.e. after damage is dealt, but before moving into the resolution step), you will gain an action point from go again during the resolution step.

You don’t retroactively get an action point from go again if you’ve passed the resolution step and you haven’t intimidated an opponent, and then you do later in the turn (e.g. after you’ve played another attack, closed the combat chain etc.)

Arc Bending’s first ability triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. When this triggered effect resolves, it will generate a replacement effect where if you were to deal damage with a Lightning or Elemental attack this combat chain, it would deal that much damage plus 1 instead. This includes damage from triggered effects the Elemental or Lightning attacks may have or even damage dealt during the damage step of combat.

Arc Bending’s second ability is a Lightning Bond ability that generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you pitched a Lightning card to play Arc Bending, Arc Bending will get go again.

You must pitch at least one Lightningcard while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Lightning Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Lightning cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Lightning card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Lightning card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

When Art of the Phoenix: War resolves, it generates a continuous effect that gives Draconic attack action cards you control +1{p} for the remainder of the turn. This will apply to any attacking or defending attack action cards currently on the combat chain as well as one that come under your control later in the turn. Additionally, you will draw 2 cards.
When resolving Art of the Phoenix: War, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. draconic attack action cards getting +1{p}, drawing cards)

Assembly Module triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: You choose whether or not to remove a steam counter from it at the time the triggered effect resolves. If you don’t remove a steam counter, you will destroy Assembly Module.
Neither player receives priority at the start of turn.
When Assembly Module’s activated ability resolves, you must search your deck for a card named Hyper Driver. That Hyper Driver will be put into the arena and then you shuffle your deck.
When resolving Assembly Module’s activated ability, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, putting into the arena, shuffling).
You can fail to find a card named Hyper Driver during the search effect. Even if you don’t find and put a Hyper Driver from your deck into the arena, you must shuffle.

Astravolt Elemental triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to discard an instant card of your choice when the triggered effect resolves. If you discard an instant card this way, you will draw a card and create an Embodiment of Lightning token.

When Bad Breath is played, you must target a hero. When Bad Breath resolves, you will intimidate that hero. It will also generate a continuous triggered effect that triggers the next time an attack you control hits this turn. When that triggered effect resolves, you will create 3/2/a Might tokens.

In a multiplayer game, if the targeted hero is no longer in the game when this card resolves, you will not intimidate or count has having intimidated someone for the purposes of the effects; however, you will still create the continuous triggered effect and you will get an action point from go again.
Basalt Boots’ ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking as long as it is face-up in the arena. If you control a Seismic Surge token, this gets +1{d}.
If you control a Seismic Surge token when this defends, then you lose that token, this will lose the +1{d}.
If a Seismic Surge token is created during the damage step and causes the if-conditional effect to become active, it will get the +1{d} but the damage will not be recalculated.

You cannot play Bear Hug if you have not yet pitched a card with 6 or more {p} this turn. Intending to pitch a card with 6 or more {p} as part of playing Bear Hug does not satisfy the condition for this restriction.

When activating Beckoning Haunt, you must target an object with the aura subtype and a cost value of X, where X is any number greater than or equal to 0. When determining how many resources you must pay as part of activating this ability, you must pay a number of resources equal to essentially twice the cost value of the targeted card plus 1. When this activated ability resolves, you will return that targeted aura card in your graveyard to your hand.

An aura with a cost value of 0 can be targeted. If the aura has an X within its cost value, treat any X in that cost value as 0 (e.g. X3 would be just 3). You cannot target an aura that does not have a cost value.

Become the Bottle’s ability triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card. You must choose which card on the combat chain when the triggered effect resolves. Become the Bottle gets the chosen card’s name.
You may choose Become the Bottle itself as it is a card on the combat chain when the triggered effect resolves.
If you choose a card with multiple names (e.g. a defending split card, an attacking card with multiple names), Become the Bottle’s will get all the names of that chosen card.

Become the Cup’s first ability is a static ability that becomes functional while playing it. It is not a triggered ability. While announcing that you are playing Become the Cup, you will choose a color and it will gain that color, in addition to its existing colors. Then as normal, you will apply effects to the card that matches the effects’ descriptions. For example, if you resolve Prismatic Leyline then play Become the Cup (red) and choose blue, that card will be both red and blue for the purpose of applying Prismatic Leyline’s continuous effects.
Become the Cup’s ability is not functional while defending.
If you do not play Become the Cup, you do not get to choose a color and it will not subsequently gain the color.
If an effect prevents you from playing a card of a certain color, you wouldn’t be able to play Become the Cup and choose that particular color as you play it.

If you are under an effect that causes cards you own to lose colors (e.g. Blanch) and you play Become the Cup, you will still be able to choose a color as you play it and Become the Cup will get that color.

Beneath the Surface’s second ability is functional while it’s a defending card on the combat chain and triggers when it’s put into your graveyard from the arena. You will turn it face-down when the triggered effect resolves.
The Watery Grave ability on Beneath the Surface is not functional while defending, hence the first ability covers the case where it defends, then it goes to the graveyard.

When Billowing Mist resolves, it generates a continuous effect that applies to your next attack this turn giving it +1{p}. It also generates a replacement effect that applies to next time you would create a card with ephemeral this turn, instead creating that many plus 1.
If a single event would create two or more different cards with ephemeral (e.g. create a Fang Strike and Slither), you would choose which of those cards to create.

Blackstone Greaves’ ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve dealt arcane damage to any living object (e.g. hero, ally), at any point during this turn, it will get +1{d} for the remainder of that turn.

Blast Rig’s Evo Upgrade ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. It gets +1{p} for each Evo you have equipped. If you equip an Evo while Blast Rig is currently on the combat chain, then it will get another +1{p}.
Blast Rig’s ability is not functional while defending.
Blessing of Bellona’s second ability triggers each time a card is put into your soul. You will create a Courage token when each of these triggered effects resolve.
Putting multiple cards into your soul at the same time will trigger Blessing of Bellona’s triggered ability that many times. For example, if you put

Blessing of Bellona’s third ability triggers at the start of your turn. You will put Blessing of Bellona into your soul when the triggered effect resolves. This can cause Blessing of Bellona’s second ability to trigger.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Blessing of Themis’ second ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). When this triggered effect resolves, you will name a card. Then you will turn all cards with that name in all banished zones face-down.
When resolving Blessing of Themis’ “when this enter the arena” triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. naming a card, turning cards face down)

When naming a card for Blessing of Themis, you may choose one of the names of a split card (e.g. Shock, Life). If you do, you will turn all cards with that name face-down. For example, Tyler names “Life” and Nic has “Everbloom||Life” and “Arcane Shock||Life” in their banished zone. Tyler will turn both “Everbloom||Life” and “Arcane Shock||Life” face-down.

Blessing of Themis’ second ability also has another connected triggered effect while it is in the arena and triggers each time a card with that name is banished. You will turn that banished card face-down when the triggered effect resolves.
Blessing of Themis’s third ability triggers at the start of your turn. You will put Blessing of Themis into your soul when the triggered effect resolves.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Blunten’s ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack with the weapon type. The attacking hero chooses and discards a card when this triggered effect resolves.

When activating Bolt’n Boots activated ability, you must target an arrow attack that currently has {p} greater than its base. When the activated ability resolves, if that targeted arrow attack still has {p} greater than its base, then it will get go again. If it does not, it will not get go again.

Bone Puppetry’s first ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you may choose and return an ally card from your graveyard to the arena when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will generate a delayed triggered effect that triggers at the beginning of the end phase. When that triggered effect resolves, you will destroy Bone Puppetry and discard your hand.
Neither player receives priority during the end phase of a turn.
Boo, Resident Spook’s second ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If Boo is untapped, it gets Spellvoid 2.

When Break Open the Chests! resolves, you will turn all cards in all arsenals face-up. Then, if there is at least one face-up yellow card among all cards in the arsenal, you will create 2 Gold tokens.
When resolving Break Open the Chests!, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. turning cards face-up, creating Gold tokens)
Bubba-Lubba’s first ability generates an identity-replacement effect that puts a +1{p} counter on it as it enters the arena. This is not a triggered effect.
When Bubba-Lubba’s third activated ability resolves, you will choose and destroy an aura token.

When Burnished Bunkerplate’s activated ability resolves, you may choose an action card from your arsenal to the active chain link as a defending card. This can cause abilities on the action card to trigger such as those that use “when this defends”.

Buzzard Helm triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, you will draw a card first, then you will discard a card at random from your hand. If the randomly discarded card has 6 or more {p} when it is discarded this way, Buzzard Helm will get +1{d} for the remainder of the turn.
If you cannot draw a card, you will still discard a card.
When resolving Buzzard Helm’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. drawing a card, discarding a card)
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.
If a card with go again is played as though it were an instant, you will gain an action point from the go again when the card resolves if it is your turn. Otherwise, you do not.

By the Book’s second ability generates a continuous effect that prevents heroes from specifically drawing cards during action phases. This does not prevent players from drawing cards during the end phase, and it does not prevent players from putting cards into their hands from effects.
By the Book’s third ability triggers at the beginning for your action phase. You will destroy By the Book when the triggered effect resolves.

Chain Brutality’s ability generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If Chain Brutality has a {p} of 6 or more, it will get go again and a triggered ability that triggers when Chain Brutality hits a hero. When that triggered effect resolves, the next attack action card you play this turn will have its base {p} set to 6 base {p}.
Effects that specify the “base {p}” of a card are applied before other effects that affect the {p} of a card.

If Chain Brutality has 5{p} or less when it hits, then it becomes 6{p} or more after you hit, Chain Brutality will still get the “when this hits a hero” triggered ability; however, it will not retroactively trigger as the ability was not active at the moment Smashing Ground hit the hero.

Go again is checked during the resolution step. If Chain Brutality has less than 6{p} and then has 6 or more {p} before the attack’s resolution step (i.e. after damage is dealt, but before moving into the resolution step), you will gain an action point from go again during the resolution step.

You don’t retroactively get an action point from go again if you’ve passed the resolution step and Chain Brutality has less than 6{p}, and then it has 6 or more {p} later in the turn (e.g. after you’ve played another attack, closed the combat chain etc.)

Channel Galcia’s Cradle first ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). It also triggers at the start of your turn. This is a targeted effect: you must target an ally, aura, item, or equipment when you add the triggered effect to the stack. When the triggered effect resolves, you will freeze that target and it will remain frozen while Channel Galcia’s Cradle is in the arena.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.
You may decide to destroy this even if you have enough Ice cards in your pitch zone. In that situation you will put your pitch zone on the bottom of your deck as usual.

When adding the triggered effect from Channel Galcia’s Cradle’s first ability to the stack, you can target an already frozen ally, aura, item or equipment. If you targeted one of these frozen objects, the freeze effect will not fail when the triggered effect resolves. The previous freeze effect’s will no longer apply once the effect’s duration is met. The new freeze effect on the targeted object will continue as long as Channel Galcia’s Cradle is in the arena.

While Channel Iceloch Glaze is a permanent in the arena, the cards in an opponent’s arsenal (cannot be played or activated) while they control a Frostbite or a frozen permanent (e.g. frozen hero, frozen ally, Frostbite in one of their equipment zones, etc.)
You may decide to destroy this even if you have enough Ice cards in your pitch zone. In that situation you will put your pitch zone on the bottom of your deck as usual.

Channel the Skybreaker’s second ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). It also triggers at the beginning of your action phase. You will create 2 Might tokens when this triggered effect resolves.
You may decide to destroy this even if you have enough Earth cards in your pitch zone. In that situation you will put your pitch zone on the bottom of your deck as usual.

Cheating Scoundrel’s first ability generates a continuous effect that applies to the next attack action card you play this turn. This continuous effect gives a +3{p} as well as a triggered ability that triggers when that attack action card resolves on the stack and becomes attacking. When that triggered effect resolves, you will wager with the defending hero. The winner of the wager creates a Gold token.

Wagers that specify the defending hero can also be set up when the attack-target is an ally. This is a rule change beginning with Compendium of Rathe. If an attack’s attack-target is an ally, the defending hero will be its controller.

Cheating Scoundrel’s second ability generates a replacement effect. The hero that wins a wager is determined when that wager’s triggered effect resolves during the resolution step. If you would lose a wager this turn, instead you may choose and discard a card. If you discard a card this way, you will win that wager instead.
If multiple wager effects were generated, this will only change the winner of one of those wagers, not all of them.

When Chorus of Rotwood resolves, it generates a Decompose effect that is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to banish 2 Earth cards and an action card from your graveyard when Chorus of Rotwood resolves. If you banish the three cards this way, you will create an Embodiment of Earth token. Regardless of if you resolve the Decompose effect or not, you will create 3 Runechant tokens.

To decompose, you need to banish 3 cards in total: two Earth cards, and one action card. The Earth cards may be action cards, and the action card may be an Earth card. Banishing an Earth action card will count towards either one of the two Earth cards being banished or the action card being banished, not both at the same time.
When resolving Chorus of Rotwood, there is no priority between any effects generated (e.g. banishing cards, creating an Embodiment of Earth, creating Runechants).
When Clearwater Elixir resolves, it generates a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. Then you may destroy a Bloodrot Pox token you control. If you do, you will gain 1{h}.
When resolving Clearwater Elixir, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. generating the continuous effect, destroying a token, gaining {h}).

When Cloud Cover resolves, it will generate a one-shot-prevention effect that prevents 3/2/1 damage from a single event of damage that would be dealt to your hero. Any remaining prevention amount does not carry over to the next time you’d be dealt damage. (e.g. If Cloud Cover’s prevention effect would prevent 3 damage, and then you’re dealt 1 damage, you will prevent the 1 damage; however, you will not prevent any more damage with the same Cloud Cover effect later that turn).

Colors of Aria only has the Earth, Ice and Lightning supertypes while it is face-up in any zone. This includes when it is pitched or banished from hand as it is made public and face-up before moving zones. This is important for abilities like bond (e.g. Earth Bond, Ice Bond, Lightning Bond) or conditional effects like those written on Lexi’s/Oldhim’s activated abilities or on cards like Elemental Strike.

Colors of Aria cannot be revealed if the reveal effect specifically requires you to reveal a specific type card. This is because Colors of Aria is still private and face-down before you perform the reveal effect and therefore will not have any of the supertypes. This is important as Colors of Aria cannot be used to pay for fusion additional costs (e.g. Lightning Fusion, etc.) nor for resolving Bravo, Star of the Show’s triggered effect.

Comeback Kicks’ first ability triggers whenever the crowd cheers you, regardless of whose turn it is. This is an optional effect: when the triggered effect resolves, you choose if you want to destroy Comeback Kicks provided you have less life than each other hero. If you destroy it this way, you will gain 1 action point.
When resolving Comeback Kicks’ triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. destroying it, gaining an action point).

Concealed Nerve Gas triggers while it is equipped face down and when an attack with go again hits you. Triggering this ability is optional: If the triggering condition is met while it is equipped face-down, you may choose to make this card public and trigger its effect. If you choose not to, the card remains private.

When Concealed Nerve Gas’ triggered effect resolves, you must destroy Concealed Nerve Gas, then you will create a Frailty token under each opponent’s control, beginning with the opponent to the left of you and moving clockwise around the table.

Concealed Pathogen triggers while it is equipped face down, an attack hits you and that attack’s controller played/activated an attack reaction this chain link. Triggering this ability is optional: If the triggering condition is met while it is equipped face-down, you may choose to make this card public and trigger its effect. If you choose not to, the card remains private.

When Concealed Pathogen’s triggered effect resolves, you must destroy Concealed Pathogen, then you will create a Bloodrot Pox token under each opponent’s control, beginning with the opponent to the left of you and moving clockwise around the table.

Concealed Sedative triggers while it is equipped face down and an attack with {p} greater than its base hits you. Triggering this ability is optional: If the triggering condition is met while it is equipped face-down, you may choose to make this card public and trigger its effect. If you choose not to, the card remains private.

When Concealed Sedative’s triggered effect resolves, you must destroy Concealed Sedative, then you will create an Inertia token under each opponent’s control, beginning with the opponent to the left of you and moving clockwise around the table.

Concoct Disorder triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, each hero puts the top card of their deck face-down into their arsenal, beginning with you and moving clockwise around the table. If 2 or more cards are put into arsenals this way, Concoct Disorder gets go again.
If a hero is unable to put a card in their arsenal, that effect fails for that hero.

When resolving Concoct Disorder’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. each hero putting the top card of their deck face-down into their arsenal, getting go again).

Conquer the Icy Terrain triggers when it hits a hero. This is an optional effect: that hero may choose whether or not to pay {r}{r} when this triggered effect resolves. If they don’t pay {r}{r} this way, you will have an optional effect: you may choose and destroy a frozen card in their arsenal or a frozen non-hero permanent they control or you may do nothing at all.

Courageous Crossing’s third ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack with {p} greater than its base. This is a targeted effect: you must target a permanent in the arena when you add the triggered effect to the stack. When the triggered effect resolves, you will remove a +1{p} counter from that targeted permanent.
You can target any permanent in the arena even if it doesn’t current have a +1{p} counter at the time you declare your target.

Crown of Everbloom’s instant-activated ability requires you to destroy it to pay for its cost. After the activated ability resolves, you must put a card from your arsenal on the bottom of your deck. If you put an arsenal card on the bottom of your deck this way, you will draw a card and create a Spellbane Aegis token.

Paying Crown of Everbloom’s activation cost means that you would no longer be able to use the Arcane Barrier ability on its card. For example, if you would be dealt 3 or more arcane damage in a single event, you cannot apply both Crown of Everbloom’s Arcane Barrier prevention effect then create a Spellbane Aegis using Crown of Everbloom’s activated ability to apply the Spellbane Aegis’ Spellvoid 1.
Crown of Everbloom’s activated ability can be activated while it is defending.

Crown of Frozen Thoughts’ first ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, you will freeze the attacking hero until the start of their next turn.

When Crown of Frozen Thoughts triggers and becomes a defending card against an attacking ally, the hero who controls the attacking ally is established as the attacking hero. This is a rule change beginning with Compendium of Rathe.

When Cut n’ Carve resolves, you will sharpen the target sword you control. Then if that sword has the prescribed number of +1{p} counters or more at that time, that sword’s next attack this turn will get dominate. If it meets the condition after Cut n’ Carve resolves, that sword’s next attack this turn will not get dominate this way.

Deep Recesses of Existence’s second ability triggers when the combat chain closes. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to banish this card face-down. If you banish a card face-down this way, then for each hero who has lost {h} this turn, you will banish a card from each of those heroes’ graveyards beginning with you then moving clockwise around the table.
Neither player receives priority during the close step of combat.

Depths of Despair’s first ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, it generates a delayed triggered effect that triggers when the combat chain closes. You will banish it when that specific triggered effect resolves.
Neither player receives priority during the close step of combat.
When Descend into Madness resolves, you will banish a random card from the defending hero’s hand, then that hero will draw a card.
If an attack’s attack-target is an ally, the defending hero will be its controller. This is a rule change beginning with Compendium of Rathe.
When resolving Descend into Madness, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. banishing a card, drawing a card)
You choose the modes and targets when you play this card. When resolving the chosen modes, they will resolve in the order printed on the card.
You may not choose the same mode more than once.
You must choose at least one of the modes or both modes. If you choose both modes, you must declare a target for each of the modes chosen as part of playing Destructive Tendencies.
If you choose both modes and all of the targets for one of the modes no longer exist, you will continue to any remaining effects and modes if able.

When Display of Craftsmanship resolves, the targeted weapon attack gets the +{p} increase. If that attack’s weapon was sharpened this turn before the Display of Craftsmanship resolves, you will put a +1{p} counter on it. If you sharpen that weapon after Display of Craftsmanship resolves, it will not get the +1{p} counter retroactively.

Distant Rumbling’s first ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). When this triggered effect resolves, you will first draw a card, then you will put a card from your hand into your deck fifth from the top.
If you cannot draw a card, you will still put a card into your deck fifth from the top.
Fifth from the top means that there are exactly four cards above the card in the deck. If there are fewer than four cards in your deck, put the card on the bottom of your deck.
When resolving Distant Rumbling’s first triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. drawing a card, putting a card into your deck).

Distant Rumbling’s second ability triggers at the beginning of your start phase. Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn. When the triggered effect resolves, you will destroy this aura then immediately create the prescribed number of Seismic Surge tokens.
The Seismic Surge’s created by the second ability will trigger in the same turn they are created because Seismic surges trigger at the start of the action phase.

Doomsaying’s second ability triggers at the beginning of your end phase. Neither player receives priority during the end phase of a turn. When the triggered effect resolves, you will put a doom counter on it, then each hero will destroy a number of auras they control equal to the number of doom counters on Doomsaying. If a hero controls less auras than the number of doom counters currently on Doomsaying, they must destroy as many as they can.
Doomsaying is an aura and is eligible to be destroyed by its triggered effect.

If you were to hit twice on the same chain link (e.g. hit with a dagger using a “Flick Knives” effect and hit with an attack during the damage step), you would be able to activate Double Cross Strap’s activated ability.
Doubling Season’s replacement effect will not apply to power increases from +{p} counters.
Effects that increase power are applied as events. This effect modifies that event (and thus the result of the effect) to increase the power by an additional 1.

This effect increases ALL instances of effects that increase power, not just the first. If two or more effects would increase the power of Doubling Season by 1, then each of those effects instead increase the power by 2.
Effects that apply when Doubling Season is played and on the stack (e.g. Come to Fight) will be affected by its ability.
Effects that set or decrease power are not modified by Doubling Season’s effect (e.g. -2{p} would not become -1{p}).

If an effect that was modified by Doubling Season were to no longer apply to an attack, but then is re-applied to that attack later, the application of Doubling Season’s replacement effect on that particular effect would be lost and no longer modify it when it is reapplied.

Drag Down triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack (e.g. weapon attack, attack action card, ally attack, etc.). That attack will get the prescribed power decrease when this triggered effect resolves.

When activating Dyed Silk Sleeves activated ability, you must target a Ninja attack action card on the active chain link. When the activated ability resolves, that Ninja attack action card will get +1{p}. Also, you will generate a conditional triggered effect that will trigger when the chain link resolves. Whether or not the attack hit is checked when the triggered effect resolves in the resolution step. If the attack did not hit, you will destroy Dyed Silk Sleeves.

Dynastic Diadem’s first ability is a static ability that generates a continuous effect that prevents Fealty tokens you control from being destroyed by opponents’ effects. If a destroy effect an opponent controls requires them to declare a target (e.g. destroy target aura), the opponent is still able to declare and target Fealty tokens you control; however, those Fealty tokens are not destroyed when the effect resolves.

Dynastic Diadem’s second ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you control 3 or more Fealty tokens, Dynastic Diadem will get +1{d}. If you control 3 Fealty tokens when this defends, then you lose one Fealty token, this will lose the +1{d}.

If a Fealty token comes under your control during the damage step and causes the if-conditional effect to become active for controlling 3 or more tokens, it will get the +1{d} but the damage will not be recalculated.

When paying the cost to play Elemental Strike, you must banish a card from your hand. If the card that you banish this way has the Earth, Lightning and/or Ice supertypes, it will get those respective continuous effects immediately. If it has none of those supertypes, nothing further happens.

If you were to banish a Colors of Aria to pay for Elemental Strike’s additional cost effect, you will get all three continuous effects. This is because it will become public and face-up in your hand before it is moved (banished).
While Embalm is in your banished zone, you may play it from your banished zone. If you play it from your banished zone this way, it gets go again.
When Embalm resolves, you will choose and put an attack action card with blood debt from your graveyard on the bottom of your deck.
When Embody Greatness resolves, you must choose and name a living legend hero. The hero you name for this effect must be for a living legend hero in the classic constructed format.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.

Energy of the Audience’s ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you have less {h} than each other hero, it gets +1{p} for each aura with the suspense ability you control. If another aura of suspense comes under your control while Energy of the Audience is currently on the combat chain, then it will get another +1{p}.
Energy of the Audience’s ability is not functional while defending.

Enflame the Firebrand’s triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. The effects generated are determined by the number of Draconic chain links you control when this triggered effect begins to resolve: this does not change while resolving the triggered effect. For example, if you have control 3 Draconic chain links and one non-Draconic chain link when this triggered effect resolves, Enflame the Firebrand gets go again and the chain link with the non-Draconic attack gets the Draconic supertype. This will cause you to control 4 Draconic chain links; however, Enflame the Firebrand will not get the +2{p} because you did not control 4 Draconic chain links when the triggered effect began to resolve.
This has the base subtype. It can be transformed into another evo.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.
This has the base subtype. It can be transformed into another evo.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.
This has the base subtype. It can be transformed into another evo.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.
This has the base subtype. It can be transformed into another evo.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.

Excessive Bloodloss’ first ability is a Contract ability that is functional while it is an attacking card on the combat chain. This ability triggers each time you complete the contract: banishing an opponent’s red card. When each of these triggered effects resolve you will create a Silver token.
Excessive Bloodloss’ Contract ability is not functional while defending.

To banish an opponent’s cards, you must be the one instructed by an effect to do the banishing. If an opponent is instructed by an effect to banish a card, it does not complete the contract because YOU are not banishing an opponent’s card.
Banishing multiple of your opponents’ red cards at the same time completes this contract that many times. (e.g. the contract is completed with each red card banished).

Excessive Bloodloss’ second ability triggers when this hits a hero. When the triggered effect resolves, you must banish the top card of that hero’s deck. If it’s red, you will repeat the process again of banishing the top card of that hero’s deck. Banishing red cards this way causes you to complete the contract and will trigger the card’s contract ability since you are the one doing the banishing.
When resolving Excessive Bloodloss’ triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. banishing, repeating the process, banishing).
Farflight Longbow’s second ability allows your arrow attacks to target any opposing hero or ally, which includes opposing heroes or allies that are not directly to the left or right of you.

Farflight Longbow’s second ability generates an effect that allows you to target any opposing hero or ally. However, if there is an effect that requires you to target a specific object if able (e.g. Arc Light Sentinel, Chum), those objects must be targeted first. This is because rules and effects that generate requirements supercede those rules and effects that generate allowances.

Feign Vengeance’s ability triggers when its chain link resolves on the stack. Whether or not there is a card defending it is checked when the triggered effect resolves. If there is a card defending it, you will draw a card.

Fire that Burns Within triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to discard a Phoenix Flame when this triggered effect resolves. If you discard a Phoenix Flame this way, you will draw a card and Fire that Burns Within will get +2{p}.
When resolving Fire that Burns Within’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. discarding, drawing a card, getting +2{p})
Fluid Motion’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you haven’t created a card this turn when you attack with this and then you do, this will get go again.

Go again is checked during the resolution step. If you haven’t created a card this turn when you attack with this and then you do before the attack’s resolution step (i.e. after damage is dealt, but before moving into the resolution step), you will gain an action point from go again during the resolution step.

You don’t retroactively get an action point from go again if you’ve passed the resolution step and you haven’t created a card, and then you do later in the turn (e.g. after you’ve played another attack on the same combat chain, etc.)
Effects that create cards in your hand or create tokens satisfy the if-conditional for Fluid Motion’s ability.

Four Feathers One Crown triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. When this triggered effect resolves, it will get +1{p} for each card with Phoenix Bannerman in its name in your graveyard at the time the triggered effect resolves. For example, if you have 3 cards with Phoenix Bannerman in its name in your graveyard when the triggered effect resolves, Four Feathers One Crown will get +3{p}. In the same scenario, if a fourth card with Phoenix Bannerman in its name were to be put in the graveyard after the triggered effect resolves, it will not get an additional +1{p} for that fourth card.

Frail Swingline’s third ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack with {p} less than its base. That attack’s controller chooses and discards a card when the triggered effect resolves.

When Frost Spike resolves, you will create a Frostbite token in an exposed head, chest, arms, or legs zone of your choice. This can be in one of your exposed zones or one of your opponent’s exposed zones.
Creating a Frostbite in an exposed zone creates it under the control of the player whose zone it is.

When Frosthaven Sheath resolves, if you pitched an Ice card to play it, you will create a Frostbite token under the attacking hero’s control. This includes the hero who controls the attacking ally. Then, it is added to the combat chain as a defending card.

You must pitch at least one Ice card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Ice Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Ice cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Ice card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Ice card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

To defend together with a card, this must become a defending card at the same time as the other card. This can either be done by the defending hero declaring both cards during the defend step of combat, or by an effect that puts both of the cards onto the chain link as defending cards at the same time.
You typically can only defend with an equipment (with a defense value) once per combat chain, but the effect lasts until the end of the turn.
Each time you defend together with Gauntlets of Unity in the same turn it gains an additional +1 defense until end of turn, but will get an -1{d} counter after each time the combat chain closes.

Gentle Breeze’s first ability is a static ability that generates a continuous effect that sets the base {p} your other attacks. Effects that specify the “base {p}” of a card/attack are applied before other effects that affect the {p} of a card/attack.
Gentle Breeze’s ability is not functional while defending.

Ghost Protocol: Architect’s second ability triggers when it resolves and becomes an attacking card. When this triggered effect resolves, you must search your deck for a card with the Evo sub-type with a cost less than or equal to the number of Evos you currently have equipped. The Evo card you searched for is banished face up and then you shuffle your deck.
When resolving Ghost Protocol: Architect’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, banishing the card, shuffling).
You can fail to find an Evo card during the search effect. Even if you don’t find and banish an Evo card from your deck, you must shuffle.

Ghost Protocol: Mainframe’s Evo Upgrade ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. It gets +1{p} for each Evo you have equipped. If you equip an Evo while Ghost Protocol: Mainframe is currently on the combat chain, then it will get another +1{p}.
Ghost Protocol: Mainframe’s second ability is not functional while defending.

Glory Plate’s first ability is always checking how many Toughness tokens have left the arena during a turn, giving it +1{d} for each token that left during that turn (e.g. destroyed from the Toughness token’s triggered ability, an effect that destroys auras, etc.).
High Tide checks your pitch zone constantly, not just when it defends. If the condition is met after its already defending, it will get the effect.

Gloves of Erasure triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). This is a targeted effect: you must target an aura token when you add the triggered effect to the stack. When the triggered effect resolves, you will destroy that targeted aura token.

When playing Glyph Destruction Nodes, you must target up to X target heroes and/or allies, where X is the number of aura permanents you control with Sigil in their name at the time you play the card. When Glyph Destruction Nodes resolves, you will deal 3 arcane damage to each of the targeted heroes and/or allies.
If you do not control any aura permanents with Sigil in its name at the time you play Glyph Destruction Nodes, X will be 0.
If any of the targeted heroes and/or allies are no longer in the arena when Glyph Destruction Nodes resolves, you will still deal 3 arcane damage to any remaining heroes and/or allies.

When playing Glyph Power Spell, you must declare a target. Whether or not you control a card with Sigil in its name is checked with Glyph Power Spell resolves. If you don’t, you will deal 4 arcane damage to the target. If you do, you will deal 6 arcane damage to the target instead of 4.

Graven Cowl’s first ability is functional while its in your graveyard and triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: you may choose to destroy 2 silver you control when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will equip it.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Graven Cowl’s second ability triggers when it is equipped from anywhere other than your graveyard. This includes triggering at the start of the game. You will put a -1{d} counter on it when the triggered effect resolves.

You may activate Graven Gaslight additional times in response to itself. If you do, you must pay the additional silver. If Graven Gaslight is already equipped to a weapon zone when its activated ability resolves, it will not be equipped again.

If you would be dealt 2 or more arcane damage in a single event, you cannot apply Spellvoid 1 , then use Graven Gaslight’s instant ability in an attempt to use Spellvoid 1 again on that same event. This is because there is no priority for you to be able to use Graven Gaslight’s instant activated ability mid-event.

Graven Gloves’ first ability is functional while its in your graveyard and triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: you may choose to destroy 2 silver you control when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will equip it.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Graven Gloves’ second ability triggers when it is equipped from anywhere other than your graveyard. This includes triggering at the start of the game. You will put a -1{d} counter on it when the triggered effect resolves.

Graven Vestment’s first ability is functional while its in your graveyard and triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: you may choose to destroy 2 silver you control when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will equip it.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Graven Vestment’s second ability triggers when it is equipped from anywhere other than your graveyard. This includes triggering at the start of the game. You will put a -1{d} counter on it when the triggered effect resolves.

Graven Walkers’ first ability is functional while its in your graveyard and triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: you may choose to destroy 2 silver you control when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will equip it.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Graven Walkers’ second ability triggers when it is equipped from anywhere other than your graveyard. This includes triggering at the start of the game. You will put a -1{d} counter on it when the triggered effect resolves.

Haboob’s second ability triggers at the start of each turn (you and your opponents’). When the triggered effect resolves, you must put a storm counter on it. Then you may destroy an ash you control for each storm counter on it. If you do not, you will destroy Haboob.
Attack action cards that are attack or defending (yours and your opponents’) will get -1{p} as long as Haboob is in the arena.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Havoc Wrap’s second ability generates several continuous effects if it is tapped. If it is tapped, cards cost {r} less to play for each hero; Havoc Wrap will not untap during the end phase; and it will get a triggered ability that triggers at the start of your turn. When this triggered effect resolves, you will destroy it.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.
This is a Wrench.

Heart Wrencher has the Wrench subtype in addition to being an attack. Effects and cards that apply to wrenches (e.g. Clamp Press) or target wrenches (e.g. Fist Pump) can interact with Heart Wrencher. Effects and cards that specifically look for wrench weapons (e.g. Construct Bank Breaker) will not work with Heart Wrencher as it is specifically looking for a card with both the weapon type and wrench sub-type.

Heavy Metal Hardcore’s first ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If an Evo has been banished from boosting this turn, it will get +1{p}.

If you boost an attack on the same combat chain after Heavy Metal Hardcore’s chain link resolves and that causes the if-conditional effect to become active, it will get the +1{p} but the damage will not be recalculated.

Helm of Safe Haven’s ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When this triggered effect resolves, you will reveal the top card of your deck. If the top card revealed this way is an attack action card, you will add that revealed attack action card to the same chain link as Helm of Safe Haven, and then you will discard a card.

If an effect prevents the revealed attack action card from being added to the chain link as a defending card (e.g. overpower, etc.), the revealed attack action card remains will remain on the top of the deck and you will still discard a card.
When resolving Helm of Safe Haven’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. revealing, adding the defending card to the chain link, discarding).

To defend together with a card, this must become a defending card at the same time as the other card. This can either be done by the defending hero declaring both cards during the defend step of combat, or by an effect that puts both of the cards onto the chain link as defending cards at the same time.
You typically can only defend with an equipment (with a defense value) once per combat chain, but the effect lasts until the end of the turn.
Each time you defend together with Helm of Unity in the same turn it gains an additional +1 defense until end of turn, but will get an -1{d} counter after each time the combat chain closes.
Herald of Victoria has an ability that can only be activated from your hand. To activate this, show Herald of Victoria to all players, then pay the cost of discarding it.
Discarding this card to its ability counts as activating an instant ability, not as playing an instant.
When Herald of Victoria’s instant-activated ability resolves, attack action card your opponents control get -1{p} while attacking or defending for the remainder of the turn.

If the phantasm ability on an attack you control triggers because it is defending a non-illusionist card with 6{p}, resolving Herald of Victoria’s instant-activated ability before the phantasm triggered effect resolves will reduce the defending card’s {p} to below 6{p}. As a result, the defending card no longer meets the phantasm ability’s state-based triggering condition and the phantasm triggered effect does not resolve.

When playing High Current Currency, you must target a non-hero permanent your opponent controls. When High Current Currency resolves, you will remove all energy counters from that targeted object. Then you will create a number of Gold tokens equal to the number of energy counters you removed.
If no energy counters are removed, you do not create any Gold tokens.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.

Hyper Inflation triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. When the triggered effect resolves, it generates a continuous effect that increases the cost to play cards by {r} for both players for the remainder of the turn.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.

Insult to Injury triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain against a hero. Whether or not you have more {h} than that hero is checked when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, Insult to Injury gets go again.

When Ion Charged resolves, it generates a continuous effect that applies to Lightning and Elemental attacks with go again for the remainder of the turn. As long as the Elemental or Lightning attack has go again, it will get +1{p}. If an effect causes the attack to lose go again, they will lose the +1{p}; however, if they were able to get go again after losing it, the +1{p} would apply again.
This card can be activated during the action phase of either player’s turn.
Turning this card face-up is part of the cost of activating this ability. If you cannot turn this card face-up because it is already face-up in the Arena, you are unable to activate this ability.
When Kimono of Layered Lessons’ activated ability resolves, put a +1{d} counter on it.
Kimono of Layered Lessons’ third ability triggers at the start of your turn. You will destroy it when this triggered effect resolves.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.

Knife Through’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you haven’t hit with a dagger yet this combat chain you when you attack with this and then you do, this will get go again.

Go again is checked during the resolution step. If you haven’t hit with a dagger yet this combat chain when you attack with this and then you do before the attack’s resolution step (i.e. after damage is dealt, but before moving into the resolution step), you will gain an action point from go again during the resolution step.

You don’t retroactively get an action point from go again if you’ve passed the resolution step and you haven’t hit with a dagger this combat chain, and then you do later in the turn (e.g. after you’ve played another attack on the same combat chain, etc.)

When Laden with Earth resolves, it will generate a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. If you pitched an Earth card to play it, you will create an Embodiment of Earth token.

You must pitch at least one Earth card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Earth Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Earth cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Earth card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Earth card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu)

When you play Laden with Frost, you must target a hero. When Laden with Frost resolves, it will generate a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. If you pitched an Ice card to play it, you will create a Frostbite under that targeted hero’s control.

You must pitch at least one Ice card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Ice Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Ice cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Ice card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Ice card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

When Laden with Lightning resolves, it will generate a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. If you pitched a Lightning card to play it, you will create an Embodiment of Lightning token.

You must pitch at least one Lightning card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Lightning Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Lightning cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch a Lightning card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Lightning card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

When playing Lay Down the Challenge, you must target a hero. You will intimidate that hero when Lay Down the Challenge resolves. Then, if that hero has strictly more cards in hand than you, you will draw a card.  If the targeted hero has no cards in their hand when the intimidate effect resolves, you will still do the subsequent if-conditional effect.
You can intimidate yourself; however, you will not draw card
When resolving Lay Down the Challenge, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by the card (e.g. intimidating, drawing a card, Go again)
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.
If a card with go again is played as though it were an instant, you will gain an action point from the go again when the card resolves if it is your turn. Otherwise, you do not.

Leave ‘Em Speechless’ second ability when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). When this triggered effect resolves, you will name a card. Then while Leave ‘Em Speechless is in the arena, the named card can’t be played from hand by any player.
Leave ‘Em Speechless’ third ability triggers at the beginning for your action phase. You will destroy Leave ‘Em Speechless when the triggered effect resolves.
When Leaven Sheath resolves, if you pitched an Earth card to play it, you will create an Embodiment of Earth token. Then, it is added to the combat chain as a defending card.

You must pitch at least one Earth card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Earth Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Earth cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Earth card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Earth card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

Lighten the Load triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to discard a card or destroy an item you control or do nothing when this triggered effect resolves. If you choose and discard a card, or choose an destroy an item you control this way, Lighten the Load will get go again.
When resolving Lighten the Load’s triggered effect, there is no priority in between any of the effects generated (e.g. discarding a card, destroying an item, getting go again).

Limbs of Lignum Vitae’s first ability generates a continuous effect that always checks the number of Earth cards in your banished zone. If this is defending with 3 Earth cards in your banished zone, and then you banish an Earth card, this gets +1{d}. If you then remove an Earth card from your banished zone, it loses the +1{d}.
Earth cards that are face-down in your banished zone do not count towards Earth cards in your banished zone.

Line Crossers’ first ability generates a continuous effect that affects the way effects that are conditioned on {h} are satisfied. If your remaining {h} is the same as a hero, any effects that are conditional on you having strictly more {h} than that hero will be satisfied. Additionally, If your remaining {h} is the same as a hero, any effects that are conditional on them having less {h} than you are satisfied

If both you and your opponent have Line Crossers equipped, if you both have the same {h} as each other, both players will meet any condition that evaluates having more, the same or less {h} than the other.

Lobotomy’s second ability triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not you want to equip an Orbitoclast from your inventory when this triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will equip an Orbitoclast from your inventory.
You can fail to find a card named Orbitoclast when resolving the triggered effect from Lobotomy’s second ability.
You must have an empty weapon zone with nothing equipped to it when the effect begins to resolve in order to equip the Orbitoclast from your inventory.

Lobotomy’s third ability triggers when it hits a hero. Whether or not you currently control an Orbitoclast is checked when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, the hero that was hit loses all hero card abilities during their next action phase. They can still gain hero abilities (e.g. Brainstorm).
When Look Within resolves, you will search your deck for a card with the Chi subtype, reveal it, shuffle, then put it on top of your deck.

When searching your deck for a Chi, you can fail to find a Chi even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put on top of your deck; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards.
When resolving Look within, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, revealing, shuffling, putting it on top of your deck)

Lunar Mirage’s first ability triggers when an attack action card with 6 or more {p} is added as a defending card to its chain link. When the triggered effect resolves, Lunar Mirage will become a copy of that defending card.

If multiple attack action cards with 6 or more {p} are added as defending cards against Lunar Mirage at the same time, Lunar Mirage’s will trigger once for each of those cards. When adding multiple of these triggered effects from Lunar to the stack, the controller of Lunar Mirage will choose which order they will go onto the stack and resolve. In this case, the Lunar Mirage attack will ultimately end up as a copy of the last triggered effect resolved.

Lunar Mirage’s first ability is an event-based triggering condition. Even if the defending attack action card with 6 or more {p} that triggered Lunar Mirage’s first ability is removed from the combat chain before the triggered effect resolves, Lunar Mirage will still become a copy of that card as last known information is used.

If a Lunar Mirage becomes a copy of a defending non-illusionist attack action card with 6 or more {p} and phantasm (e.g. Embermaw Cenapai (red) with no classes or talents supertypes), phantasm will trigger in this situation. This is because the phantasm ability’s state-based triggering condition is met when the Lunar Mirage object becomes a copy of the defending attack action card with 6 or more {p}.

Magmatic Carapace’s first ability triggers whenever you play an aura. This is an optional effect: you choose to tap it and pay {r} when the triggered effect resolves. If you tap it and pay a resource this way, you will create a Seismic Surge token.
Because it is an optional effect, you must be able to both tap Magmatic Carapace and pay {r}. If you cannot do both, you cannot choose to do the optional effect.
When resolving Magmatic Carapace’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. tapping it, paying a resource, creating a token).

Man Overboard triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card. This is an optional effect: you choose whether or not you want to discard a card with the ally subtype from your hand when this triggered effect resolves. If you do, Man Overboard gets +1{p} and go again.

Manifest Muscle’s ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve created a card this turn, it will get +1{p}.
Manifest Muscle’s ability is not functional while defending.

If you create a card after damage calculation in the damage step passes in Manifest Muscle’s chain link and that causes the if-conditional effect to become active, it will get the +1{p} but the damage will not be recalculated.
Effects that create cards in your hand or create tokens satisfy the if-conditional for Manifest Muscle’s ability.
The value of X for spellvoid is evaluated at the time Spellvoid is applied as a replacement effect.

Mind Meets Might’s first ability triggers when this hits a hero. When this triggered effect resolves, that hero must reveal their hand and they discard all cards with 6 or more {p} from the revealed set of cards.
