Then, that hero will draw that many cards equal to the number of cards discarded this way.
If an effect prevents you from revealing cards in your hand, no cards are discarded and no cards are drawn from the subsequent effects.

Mist Hunter’s first ability is a Contract ability that is functional while it is an attacking card on the combat chain. This ability triggers each time you complete the contract: banishing an opponent’s blue card. When each of these triggered effects resolve you will create a Silver token.
Mist Hunter’s Contract ability is not functional while defending.

To banish an opponent’s cards, you must be the one instructed by an effect to do the banishing. If an opponent is instructed by an effect to banish a card, it does not complete the contract because YOU are not banishing an opponent’s card.
Banishing multiple of your opponents’ blue cards at the same time completes this contract that many times. (e.g. the contract is completed with each blue card banished).

Mist Hunter’s second ability triggers when this hits a hero with the Mystic talent supertype. When the triggered effect resolves, you must search their deck for any number of cards named Inner Chi and you banish them. Then, the opponent must shuffle their deck. Banishing Inner Chi cards this way causes you to complete the contract and will trigger the card’s contract ability since you are the one doing the banishing.
If you choose not to banish any cards, that opponent must still shuffle.
When resolving Mist Hunter’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, banishing, shuffling).
Mistborn Protector’s ability is functional while it’s defending and generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve created a card this turn, it will get +1{d}.
Damage will not be recalculated if the chain link where Mistborn Protector is defending passes damage calculation in the damage step and you create a card for the first time this turn.
You choose the modes and targets when you play this card. When resolving the chosen modes, they will resolve in the order printed on the card.
You may not choose the same mode more than once.
You must choose at least one of the modes. For every mode that you choose, you must declare a target for each of the modes chosen as part of playing Monolith of Galcia.
If you choose more than 1 mode and all of the targets for one of the modes no longer exist, you will continue to any remaining effects and modes if able.

Mournful Casket’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If an object with the ally subtype has been put into your graveyard at any point during this turn (e.g. destroyed from the top of your deck, discarded from your hand, destroyed while it was in the arena, etc.), Mournful Casket will get +1{d} for the remainder of that turn.

If you control a Gold (including those that count as gold), Myrkhellir Helm gets +1{d}. If you control a Gold when this defends, then you lose that Gold and control no other Gold, this will lose the +1{d} gained.
If a Gold comes under your control during the damage step and causes the if-conditional effect to become active, it will get the +1{d} from controlling it but the damage will not be recalculated.

When Myrkhellir Helm’s activated ability resolves, it generates a replacement effect that applies to the next time you draw a card using the Gold token’s activated ability this turn. If you were to draw a card from a Gold token while this replacement effect is active, you instead draw two cards.

Overcharge’s first ability generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve played an instant card during the same chain link where Overcharge is the attack, Overcharge will get +3/+2/+1{p}.
Overcharge’s first ability is not functional while defending.

When playing Painful Premonition, you must declare a target. When Painful Premonition resolves, you will deal 3/2/1 arcane damage to the target. Then, if Painful Premonition dealt damage, you will create a Sigil of Fate token.
When Phoenix Bannerman: Arms resolves, you will search your deck for a card named Phoenix Flame, reveal it, put it into your hand, then shuffle. Then, you will create a Might token.

When searching your deck for a Phoenix Flame, you can fail to find a Phoenix Flame even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put in your hand; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards and you will still create a Might token.
When resolving Phoenix Bannerman: Arms, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, revealing, putting it into your hand, shuffling, creating a token).
When Phoenix Bannerman: Chest resolves, you will search your deck for a card named Phoenix Flame, reveal it, put it into your hand, then shuffle. Then, you will create a Vigor token.

When searching your deck for a Phoenix Flame, you can fail to find a Phoenix Flame even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put in your hand; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards and you will still create a Vigor token.
When resolving Phoenix Bannerman: Chest, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, revealing, putting it into your hand, shuffling, creating a token).
When Phoenix Bannerman: Head resolves, you will search your deck for a card named Phoenix Flame, reveal it, put it into your hand, then shuffle. Then, you will create a Ponder token.

When searching your deck for a Phoenix Flame, you can fail to find a Phoenix Flame even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put in your hand; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards and you will still create a Ponder token.
When resolving Phoenix Bannerman: Head, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, revealing, putting it into your hand, shuffling, creating a token).
When Phoenix Bannerman: Legs resolves, you will search your deck for a card named Phoenix Flame, reveal it, put it into your hand, then shuffle. Then, you will create an Agility token.

When searching your deck for a Phoenix Flame, you can fail to find a Phoenix Flame even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put in your hand; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards and you will still create an Agility token.
When resolving Phoenix Bannerman: Legs, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. searching, revealing, putting it into your hand, shuffling, creating a token).
You choose the modes and targets when you play this card. When resolving the chosen modes, they will resolve in the order printed on the card.
You may not choose the same mode more than once.
You must choose at least one of the modes or both modes. If you choose both modes, you must declare a target for each of the modes chosen as part of playing Pilfer the Tomb.
If you choose both modes and all of the targets for one of the modes no longer exist, you will continue to any remaining effects and modes if able.

For Pilfer the Tomb’s first modal ability, when playing Pilfer the Tomb, you must target a card with the instant type. Targeting a card with an instant activated ability but no instant type in its type box is not a valid target.

To defend together with a card, this must become a defending card at the same time as the other card. This can either be done by the defending hero declaring both cards during the defend step of combat, or by an effect that puts both of the cards onto the chain link as defending cards at the same time.
You typically can only defend with an equipment (with a defense value) once per combat chain, but the effect lasts until the end of the turn.
Each time you defend together with Pillar of Unity in the same turn it gains an additional +1 defense until end of turn, but will get an -1{d} counter after each time the combat chain closes.

To defend together with a card, this must become a defending card at the same time as the other card. This can either be done by the defending hero declaring both cards during the defend step of combat, or by an effect that puts both of the cards onto the chain link as defending cards at the same time.
You typically can only defend with an equipment (with a defense value) once per combat chain, but the effect lasts until the end of the turn.
Each time you defend together with Plating of Unity in the same turn it gains an additional +1 defense until end of turn, but will get an -1{d} counter after each time the combat chain closes.

When Pound of Flesh resolves, each hero will banish a card from their hand, beginning with you then moving clockwise around the table. Then, each hero who didn’t banish a card with 6 or more {p} this way loses 1 {h}.

Power of Make Believe’s ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. It gets +1{p} for each card with 6 or more {p} defending it. If a card with 6 or more {p} is defending it then another one gets added to the combat chain on its chain link, Power of Make Believe will get +2{p} total from its effect.
You can only activate if you control a card with 6 or more {p}. You control cards in the arena or on the stack.
You cannot activate this ability while in the process of playing a card with 6 or more {p} to pay its resource cost.
When playing Put on Ice, you must target up to 3/2/1 target allies. When Put on Ice resolves, you will freeze those targeted allies until the start of your next turn.
You can choose not to target any allies. The remaining effects on the card will still be generated when the Put on Ice resolves
If any of the targeted allies are no longer in the arena when Put on Ice resovles, you will freeze any remaining targeted allies and draw a card (if you played Put on Ice from arsenal).
If all targeted allies are no longer in the arena when Put on Ice resolves, you will still draw a card (if you played Put on Ice from arsenal)
When resolving Put on Ice, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. freezing allies, drawing a card)

Quickening Sand’s third ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack with go again. This is a targeted effect: you must target a hero or ally when you add the triggered effect to the stack. When the triggered effect resolves, you will tap that targeted hero or ally.

Rainbow Goo Trap triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain against an attack with {p} greater than its and also has the dominate and go again abilities. When the triggered effect resolves, that attack will get -2{p} and will lose and can’t gain abilities.

When playing Ransack and Raze, you must target a landmark with a cost value of exactly X, where X is the number of resources you intend to pay for Ransack and Raze’s base cost value. When Ransack and Raze resolves, you will destroy the targeted landmark and you will create Gold tokens equal to the value of X.
If the targeted landmark no longer exists when Ransack and Raze resolves, you will still create X Gold tokens.
When resolving Ransack and Raze, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. destroy the landmark, creating Gold tokens.)
When Reach Beyond the Grave’s activated ability resolves, you will choose and return an ally card from your graveyard to your hand, then you will choose and discard a card.
If no ally cards are available to return from your graveyard to your hand when this resolves, you will still discard a card.
When resolving Reach Beyond the Grave’s activated ability, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. returning an ally card, discarding a card).
The cost value of X is determined at the time the card is declared to be played so that X resources can be paid, and the cost value for the purposes of effects (e.g. Frost Lock).

When Recede to Mistform resolves, you will choose X equipment with the cloaked ability you have equipped, where X is equal to the cost value declared when playing the card. You will turn those chosen equipment face-down.
You can declare the value of X = 0 when playing Recede to Mistform. If you do, you do not turn any equipment face-down.

When Recede to Mistform resolves, you must choose X number of equipment with cloaked if able.  If the number of equipment with cloaked is less than the value of X, you must choose all of the available equipment with cloaked.

When objects in the arena are turned from face-up to face-down, they will still count as the same object and will retain any counters on it when turned face-down (e.g. any -1{d} counters, energy counters, etc.). This is a rule change beginning with Compendium of Rathe.

Reckless Arithmetic’s ability triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain. You will roll a six-sided die when this triggered effect resolves. After the die roll is completed, Reckless Arithmetic will gain +x{p}, where X is the die roll’s result.

When Rend Flesh resolves, it generates a continuous triggered effect that is active for the remainder of the turn and triggers each time a sword you control hits a hero. Each of these triggered effects are optional effects: you may choose to remove a +1{p} counter from the sword that hit when the triggered effect resolves. If you do remove that counter from it, the hero who was hit will lose 2{h}.

When resolving the triggered effects from Rend Flesh’s continuous triggered effect, the life loss effect is not considered damage and it will not trigger other effects that are conditional on hitting.
When Restvine Elixir resolves, it generates a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. Then you may destroy an Inertia token you control. If you do, you will gain 1{h}.
When resolving Restvine Elixir, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. generating the continuous effect, destroying a token, gaining {h}).

When resolving Rip Off the Top, you will draw a card first, then you will pitch a card randomly from your hand. If you successfully pitch a card this way, you will gain resource points equal to the pitched card’s value and if that pitched card has 6 or more {p}, your next attack this turn (e.g. weapon attack, ally attack, attack action card, etc.) will get +3{p}.

When you pitch cards from resolving Rip Off the Top, it can still trigger effects due to pitching and you still gain the resources from pitching (e.g. you would gain 3 resources if it was a blue card, etc).
When resolving Rip Off the Top, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by the card (e.g. drawing a card, pitching a random card)
If you cannot draw a card, you will still randomly pitch a card.

If a card that is being pitched is in a private state, it is shown and made public first before it is moved to the pitch zone. If you are under an effect that says you can’t pitch a specific card (e.g. Chains of Eminence) you will not be able to pitch that card and it will remain in your hand. In this scenario, if you could not pitch the card and it has 6 or more {p}, you will not generate the if-conditional continuous effect and it will not apply to your next attack this turn.
This card can be activated during the action phase of either player’s turn.
Turning this card face-up is part of the cost of activating this ability. If you cannot turn this card face-up because it is already face-up in the Arena, you are unable to activate this ability.
When Rippling Wave’s activated ability resolves, you may choose a defending blue attack action card and return it to its owner’s hand.

Rites of Earthlore’s first ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). You will create a Seismic Surge token when this triggered effect resolves.

Rites of Earthlore’s second ability triggers at the beginning of your start phase. Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn. When the triggered effect resolves, you will destroy this aura. You will also generate a continuous effect that applies to the next Guardian attack action card you play this turn, giving it the prescribed increase in power.

When Roaring Beam resolves, you will create a Courage token. Then if there are no cards in your soul, you will return Roaring Beam to its owner’s hand, then you will charge your soul with a card of your choice from your hand.
When resolving Roaring Beam, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. creating a Courage token, returning the card to its owner’s hand, charging your soul.)
Robe of Resourcefulness triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). You will gain {r}{r} when the triggered effect resolves.
Rockyard Rodeo’s ability is functional at all times, regardless of the visibility or zone. Its power is defined by its ability. Outside of the game its power is considered to be 0.

If you attack/defend with this, and you lose control of your weapon with the highest base {p}, then its power will reflect the highest base {p} among any remaining weapons you control. If you no longer control any weapons or if your weapon does not have a power property, Rockyard Rodeo’s base power will be 0.

Rune Snare’s third ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain and the attacking hero has played or created 2 or more auras this turn. You will choose and destroy an object with the aura subtype they control when the triggered effect resolves.

Runeblade Robe instant-activated ability requires you to destroy it and a Runechant you control. After the activated ability resolves, you can apply its prevention effect to the next 1 arcane damage that would be dealt to you this turn.
When paying Runebleed Robe’s cost, you can destroy an object with the name Runechant that you control.

Paying Runebleed Robe’s activation cost means that you would no longer be able to use the Arcane Barrier ability on its card. For example, if you would be dealt 2 or more arcane damage in a single event, you cannot apply both Runebleed Robe’s Arcane Barrier prevention effect then the prevention effect from its activated ability to that same event.
Runebleed Robe’s activated ability can be activated while it is defending.

Runic Fellingsong triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card. This is an optional targeted effect: you must target a hero when you add the triggered effect to the stack; however, you choose whether or not to banish an aura from your graveyard when the triggered effect resolves. If you banish an aura from your graveyard this way, you will deal 1 arcane damage to that targeted hero.

With optional targeted effects like this one, you may choose not to target a hero when adding Runic Fellingsong’s triggered effect to the stack. If you do not target a hero, none of the effects banishing an aura or dealing damage are generated.
When resolving Runic Fellingsong’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by the card (e.g. banishing an aura card, dealing arcane damage).
When Sapwood Elixir resolves, it generates a continuous effect that gives your next attack this turn +3{p}. Then you may destroy a Frailty token you control. If you do, you will gain 1{h}.
When resolving Sapwood Elixir, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. generating the continuous effect, destroying a token, gaining {h}).

Savage Claw’s second ability generates a conditional continuous effect that checks what card was pitched when paying the activation cost of the preceding attack ability. If a card with 6 or more {p} was pitched to attack this way, the attack will get +1{p}.

When activating Scuttle Toes’s activated ability, you must target an ally you control. When Scuttle Toes’ activated ability resolves, you will untap that targeted ally. It will also generate a delayed triggered effect that triggers at the beginning of the end phase.You will destroy that ally when that triggered effect resolves.
Neither player receives priority during the end phase of a turn.
When resolving Scuttle Toes’ activated ability, if the targeted ally is already untapped, the untap effect will fail. You will still destroy that ally at the beginning of the end phase.
When Seeds of Strength resolves, you will create 3 Might tokens. If you pitched an Earth card to play it, you will create 4 Might tokens instead.

You must pitch at least one Earth card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Earth Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Earth cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Earth card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Earth card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

Seeker Kunai’s second ability is functional while its in your graveyard and triggers at the start of your turn. This is an optional effect: you may choose to destroy 2 silver you control when the triggered effect resolves. If you do, you will return it to the arena.
Neither player receives priority during the start phase of a turn.
The additional cost value of tapping X Seismic Surge tokens is determined at the time the card is declared to be played. This is so that X targets can be declared for Seismic Shift’s second ability.

You must have a target to play Seismic Shift for any X>0 when paying its additional cost. E.g. You cannot play Seismic Shift tap 4 Seismic Surge tokens for its additional cost, if there are not at least 4 aura token targets.
You cannot target non-token auras with Seismic Shift.
As Seismic Surge tokens have the aura sub-type, you can tap to pay for the additional cost and also target them as well.

You can still play Seismic Shift if you declare X=0. Doing so means that you will not tap any Seismic Surge tokens you control when paying the additional cost. Additionally, when Seismic Shift resolves, you will not destroy any aura tokens. You will gain an action point from resolving go again.
If all targeted aura tokens are destroyed before Seismic Shift resolves, you will still gain an action point from go again.

When Sense Weakness resolves, you will generate a continuous effect that will apply to the next Guardian attack this turn (e.g. Guardian weapon attack, Guardian attack action card). When applied, that attack will get a +1{p}, dominate and a “when this hits a hero” triggered ability.

The triggered ability from Sense Weakness’ continuous effect triggers when the Guardian attack it’s applied to hits a hero. When this triggered effect resolves, you will deal damage to each ally that the hero controls equal to the amount of damage dealt to that hero. Dealing damage to each ally from resolving this triggered effect is not considered hitting those allies for the purposes of cards and effects.

Serpent’s Kiss’ second ability triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes attacking. When this triggered effect resolves, you will choose to either create a Fang Strike or Slither in your hand; however, if you’ve transcended this turn before the triggered effect resolves, you will create both cards in your hand instead.
Serpent’s Kiss’ third ability triggers when it hits a hero. When this triggered effect resolves, you will look at the top 2 cards of that hero’s deck, then you will banish 1 of those cards.
When resolving Serpent’s Kiss’ triggered effects, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by each triggered effect.

Shallow Water Shark Harpoon’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve activated a cannon this turn, Shallow Water Shark Harpoon gets a triggered ability that triggers when it hits a hero. You will choose and destroy a card in their arsenal when this triggered effect resolves. If you destroy a card in that hero’s arsenal this way, you will create a Gold token.
When resolving the Shallow Water Shark Harpoon’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. destroying a card in arsenal, creating a Gold token)

While it is attacking on the combat chain, Shapeless Form triggers each time you play an attack action card with the ephemeral ability. You will choose a name when that triggered effect resolves. The attack action card gets that chosen name in addition to its original name.
You choose the modes and targets when you play this card. When resolving the chosen modes, they will resolve in the order printed on the card.
You may not choose the same mode more than once.
You must choose at least one of the modes or both modes. If you choose both modes, you must declare a target for each of the modes chosen as part of playing Shatter Sorcery.
If you choose both modes and all of the targets for one of the modes no longer exist, you will continue to any remaining effects and modes if able.

For Shatter Sorcery’s first modal ability, when playing Shatter Sorcery, you must target an aura permanent with Sigil in its name. Cards with the aura subtype on the stack or defending on the combat chain are not valid targets.

Shield Beater’s second ability generates a continuous effect that applies to cards you own named “Visit Anvilheim”, giving those cards go again. If your opponent plays a Visit Anvilheim you own (e.g. Inflitrate), that Visit Anvilheim will get go again.

Shimmering Mirage’s first ability is functional while its a defending card on the combat chain and triggers when the chain link it is on resolves. You will banish it when this triggered effect resolves. You may play it again this combat chain from the banished zone.

Shimmering Specter’s first ability is functional when it is in the arena as either an attacking card or defending card on the combat chain and triggers when it leaves the arena. You will create a Spectral Shield token when this triggered effect resolves.
Shroud of the Fate Watcher triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). You will create a Sigil of Fate token when the triggered effect resolves.
Sigil of Fate’s first ability triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). You will opt 1 when this triggered effect resolves..
Tokens cease to exist when they leave the arena.
Sigil of Fate’s second ability triggered at the beginning of your action phase. You will destroy it when this triggered effect resolves.

Sigil of Gravespaning’s second abilities trigger each time an object with the aura subtype leaves your graveyard (e.g put from your graveyard into your hand/on the bottom of your deck, banished, etc.). This is a targeted effect: you must target a hero when you add the triggered effect to the stack. When the triggered effect resolves, you will deal 1 arcane damage to that targeted hero.
Sigil of Gravespawning’s second ability will not trigger while it is in the graveyard as its ability is not functional.
Sigil of Gravespawning’s third ability triggers at the beginning of your action phase. You will destroy it when this triggered effect resolves.

Destroying aura tokens (e.g. Runechants, Quicken, etc.) will not cause Sigil of Gravespawning to trigger as the token ceases to exist when it is destroyed instead of being put into the graveyard (and then ceases to exist).

Sigil of Silphidae first ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). It also triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). This is an optional targeted effect: you must target a hero when you add the triggered effect to the stack; however, you choose whether or not to banish another aura from your graveyard when the triggered effect resolves. If you banish another aura from your graveyard this way, you will deal 1 arcane damage to that targeted hero.

With optional targeted effects like this one, you may choose not to target a hero when adding Sigil of Silphidae’s triggered effect to the stack. If you do not target a hero, none of the effects of banishing an aura or dealing damage are generated.
If Sigil of Silphidae triggers from leaving the aura, that Sigil of Silphidae cannot be chosen and banished from resolving its triggered effect.
Sigil of Silphidae’s first ability is not functional while it’s defending. It will not trigger this ability when it leaves the arena while it’s defending on the combat chain.
When resolving Sigil of Silphidae’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by the card (e.g. banishing an aura card, dealing arcane damage).
Sigil of Silphidae’s third ability triggers at the beginning of your action phase. You will destroy it when this triggered effect resolves.

Sigil of Voltaris’ first ability triggers when it enters the arena (e.g. resolves on the stack or when it is put into the arena from another card’s effect). It also triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). This is a targeted effect: you must target a hero when you add the triggered effect to the stack. You will deal 1 arcane damage to that targeted hero when the triggered effect resolves.
Sigil of Voltaris’ first ability is not functional while it is defending. It will not trigger this ability when it leaves the arena while it’s defending on the combat chain.
Sigil of Silphidae’s second ability triggers at the beginning of your action phase. You will destroy it when this triggered effect resolves.
Silken Shawl triggers when it is destroyed. You will create a Vigor token when the triggered effect resolves.

Destroying Silken Shawl as part of using and applying its Ward 1 prevention effect can cause it to trigger its triggered ability. You will add this triggered effect to the stack the next time effects can be added to the stack (typically before players receive priority).
Silken Shroud triggers when it is destroyed. You will create a Ponder token when the triggered effect resolves.

Destroying Silken Shroud as part of using and applying its Ward 1 prevention effect can cause it to trigger its triggered ability. You will add this triggered effect to the stack the next time effects can be added to the stack (typically before players receive priority).
Silken Slippers triggers when it is destroyed. You will create an Agility token when the triggered effect resolves.

Destroying Silken Slippers as part of using and applying its Ward 1 prevention effect can cause it to trigger its triggered ability. You will add this triggered effect to the stack the next time effects can be added to the stack (typically before players receive priority).
Silken Symphony triggers when it is destroyed. You will create a Might token when the triggered effect resolves.

Destroying Silken Symphony as part of using and applying its Ward 1 prevention effect can cause it to trigger its triggered ability. You will add this triggered effect to the stack the next time effects can be added to the stack (typically before players receive priority).

Skera Strapping’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve pitched a card with 6 or more {p} at any point during this turn, it will get spellvoid 3 for the remainder of that turn.

If you pitch a card with 6 or more {p} to use a prevention effect such as arcane barrier to reduce the damage of a single arcane damage event, you can use the spellvoid 3 from Skera Strapping to reduce any remaining arcane damage (3 or less) from that same event.

Skywarden no.161803’s Galvanize ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: You choose whether or not to destroy an item you control at the time the triggered effect resolves. If an item you control is destroyed, this gets +1{d}. If the item you destroyed this way was a Golden Cog, you will create a Gold token.
When resolving Skywarden no.161803’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. choosing to destroy an item you control, getting +1{d}, creating a Gold token).
You cannot do the optional effect if you are unable to destroy an item you control.

Smoldering Scales’ first ability generates a replacement effect. If a single event would occur where 1 or more Frostbite tokens would be created under your control, you may destroy Smoldering Scales instead.

When playing Smoldering Steel, you must target a dagger attack. When Smoldering Steel resolves, that dagger attack will get +1{p} and will get a triggered ability that triggers when the dagger attack hits a hero. When that triggered effect resolves, you will deal 1 damage to that hero.

Smoldering Steel’s second ability is functional only while it is in your graveyard and generates a replacement effect. If a single event would occur where 1 or more Frostbite tokens would be created under your control, you may banish Smoldering Steel instead.

Snarky Prick triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card on the combat chain against a hero. When the triggered effect resolves, you will look at the top card of that hero’s deck. If the top card you looked at this way is red, you will destroy it and Snarky Prick gets +4{p}.
Destroying the top card of a deck will not trigger any “when this is destroyed,…” effects unless it is specified by the ability’s triggering condition.

Destroying the top card of a deck will not satisfy any condition nor trigger any effects that look at whether you’ve destroyed an object you control (e.g. Merciful Retribution, Ratchet Up) as players do not control the cards in their decks.
When resolving Snarky Prick’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. looking, destroying, getting +4{p}).

Solforge Gauntlet’s first ability triggers when the combat chain closes. Whether or not you defended with it this turn is checked when the triggered effect resolves. If it did, you will put it into your soul.
Neither player receives priority during the close step of combat.

Solray Plating’s first ability generates a prevention effect that can be used once each time you would be dealt damage. If you would be dealt damage, you may banish a card from your soul to prevent 1 of that damage. If you do, banish a card from your soul this way to use the prevention effect, you will destroy this at the beginning of the end phase.
Neither player receives priority during the end phase of a turn.
When Song of Larinkmorth White resolves, you will create a Frostbite token under each other hero’s control, beginning with the hero on your left and moving clockwise around the table.
If an effect reduces the number tokens created (e.g. Ripple Away), no Frostbite tokens will be created from resolving this effect.

Soul Bond Belief triggers when it resolves on the stack and becomes an attacking card. You will reveal the top card of your deck when this triggered effect resolves. If the revealed card is yellow, you will put that revealed card into your soul and Soul Bond Belief gets +1{p}.

When Sowing Thorns resolves, you will gain 1{h}. Then it generates a Decompose effect that is an optional effect: you choose whether or not to banish 2 Earth cards and an action card from your graveyard when Sowing Thorns resolves. If you banish the three cards this way, you may search your deck for an Earth aura with cost less than the total {h} you’ve gained this turn. You will reveal that card, then shuffle and put it on top.

If you choose to search your deck for a Earth aura card, you can fail to find an Earth aura card even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put on top; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards.

To decompose, you need to banish 3 cards in total: two Earth cards, and one action card. The Earth cards may be action cards, and the action card may be an Earth card. Banishing an Earth action card will count towards either one of the two Earth cards being banished or the action card being banished, not both at the same time.
When resolving Sowing Thorns, there is no priority between any effects generated (e.g. gaining {h}, banishing cards, searching, revealing, shuffling, putting on top).

Speed Demon’s second ability is functional when it’s in the arena and attacking on the combat chain. It generates a continuous effect that is always checking. If you control a Hyper Driver, it will get +1{p}. If you control a Hyper Driver when this attacks, then you lose it, this will lose the +1{p}.
You do not control Hyper Drivers that are sub-cards under objects you control in the arena.

If a Hyper Driver comes under your control during the damage step and causes the if-conditional effect to become active, it will get the +1{p} from controlling it but the damage will not be recalculated.
Speed Demon’s second ability is not functional while defending.

Speed Demon’s third ability triggers when it resolves and becomes an attacking card. Whether or not you scrapped a Hyper Driver as part of paying this card’s Scrap additional cost is checked when the triggered effect resolves. If you did, you will create a Hyper Driver token with 2 steam counters.

Spellbane Trap triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain and the attacking hero has dealt arcane damage this turn (e.g. to you, themselves, another opposing hero, ally you control, etc.) You will create a Spellbane Aegis token when the triggered effect resolves.

When Spreading Mist resolves, it generates a continuous effect that applies to your next attack this turn giving it go again. It also generates a replacement effect that applies to next time you would create a card with ephemeral this turn, instead creating that many plus 1.
If a single event would create two or more different cards with ephemeral (e.g. create a Fang Strike and Slither), you would choose which of those cards to create.

Sprout Strength (Red) generates 3 separate continuous effects that apply to your next attack with each of them giving that next attack +1{p}. If you had a replacement effect that modifies the effects that cause attacks to gain power this turn (e.g. Thrive), it would modify each of those effects.

Sprout Strength (Yellow) generates 2 separate continuous effects that apply to your next attack with each of them giving that next attack +1{p}. If you had a replacement effect that modifies the effects that cause attacks to gain power this turn (e.g. Thrive), it would modify each of those effects.

Stadium Security’s ability is functional while it’s in your arsenal and generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve controlled a Toughness token at any point during this turn, Stadium Security gets ambush for the remainder of that turn.

When Stormweaver’s Aegis’ instant activated ability resolves, all cards with the instant type in its type box you own get an instant activated ability for the remainder of the turn. The instant activated ability that these cards get can only be activated from your hand. To activate this, you will show the instant card to all players, then pay the cost of discarding it. When that activated ability resolves, it generates a shielding-prevention effect that prevents the next 2 damage that would be dealt to your hero. This can be over separate events of damage (i.e. 1 damage, then 1 damage).
Discarding this card to pay the cost of activating ability given to instant cards from Stormweaver’s Aegis counts as activating an instant ability, not as playing an instant.
Cards without the instant type in your hand that can be played as though it were an instant are not instant cards and would not get this ability from Stormweaver’s Aegis.
When Stormwind Sheath resolves, if you pitched a Lightning card to play it, you will create an Embodiment of Lightning token. Then, it is added to the combat chain as a defending card.

You must pitch at least one Lightning card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Lightning Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Lightning cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch a Lightning card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Lightning card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.
If a card with go again is played as though it were an instant, you will gain an action point from the go again when the card resolves if it is your turn. Otherwise, you do not.

When playing Submerge, it has an additional effect cost that requires you to put a card from your hand into your deck fifth from the top. Fifth from the top means that there are exactly four cards above the card in the deck. If there are fewer than four cards remaining in your deck, you will put it on the bottom of your deck and you have paid the additional cost.
Swordmaster’s Shine’s ability reduces the cost of playing it by {r} for each +1{p} counter on all cards with the sword subtype you control.
Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs.

When paying the cost to activate Synapse Sparkcap’s activated ability, you must tap it and banish an object with the Evo subtype from your hand. When the activated ability resolves, you will create a Ponder token.

You cannot banish Singularity from your hand even though Teklovossen, the Mechropotent is its back-face. Even though Teklovossen, the Mechropotent has the Evo subtype, that face of the card is not its active face while it’s in your hand.

Teklo Trebuchet 2000’s ability triggers when it resolves and becomes an attacking card. When this triggered effect resolves, it generates a continuous effect that applies to the next attack you boost this combat chain, giving it +2{p}.

Tempest Dancers triggers when it leaves the arena (e.g. when it is destroyed, when it is banished, etc.). When this triggered effect resolves, it will generate a continuous effect that will allow you to play your next non-attack action card this turn as though it were an instant. If you choose to not use this effect when you play your next non-attack action card this turn, you won’t be able to use that effect on your next attack action card you play after that in the same turn.
An action card played as though it were an instant does not cost an action point and can be played whenever the player has priority, otherwise it is still considered to be an action card.
If a card with go again is played as though it were an instant, you will gain an action point from the go again when the card resolves if it is your turn. Otherwise, you do not.

When Templar Spellbane’s instant activated ability resolves, it generates a shielding-prevention effect that prevents the next 1 arcane damage (or 2 arcane damage total if you’ve activated a weapon this turn) that would be dealt to your hero. This can be over separate events of damage (i.e. 1 arcane damage, then 1 arcane damage).

To get the additional value for the shielding-prevention effect, you need to have activated a weapon this turn before Templar Spellbane’s activated ability resolves. If you do, prevent the next 2 arcane damage that would be dealt to you instead of just 1.

When playing Temporal Wobble, you must target a non-attack action card with a cost value less than the number of aura permanents you control with Sigil in their name at the time you play the card. Because the negate effect requires a target non-attack action card, this target must be a non-attack action card that is currently waiting on the stack to resolve. When Temporal Wobble resolves, you will negate the targeted non-attack action card. Then, the controller of that negated non-attack action card will gain 1 action point.

If the targeted non-attack action card is no longer on the stack when Temporal Wobble resolves, its controller still gains 1 action point as you will continue to resolve any of the remaining non-connected effects on Temporal Wobble on the card.

When Tentacular Toll resolves, you will choose and turn up to 3/2/1 ally cards in your graveyard face-down. Then you will create Gold tokens equal to the number to ally cards you turned face-down this way.
You can choose not to turn any ally cards in your graveyard face-down when this resolves. If you don’t turn any ally cards face-down this way, no Gold tokens are created.
When resolving Tentacular Toll, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. Turning ally cards face-down, creating Gold tokens).

Tiger Trap triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain and the attacking hero currently controls 3 or more attacks with {p} greater than their base. When the triggered effect resolves, it will generate a continuous effect that will prevent attacks they control from gaining {p} for the remainder of this turn. This includes any attacks currently on the combat chain as well as any future attacks this turn.

When determining whether or not the attacking hero currently controls 3 or more attacks {p} greater than their base, you will count the current number of attacks with {p} greater than their base on the combat chain. This includes attack action cards or attacks currently being represented by their objects (e.g. weapons, allies). Attacks that are no longer on their chain links such as those that were removed from the combat chain (e.g. Endless Arrow, Double Strike) or a previous weapon/ally attack where the weapon/ally is no longer representing the attack on the chain link do not count in this determination.

This will remove any +{p} effects currently modifying  any remaining attacks on previous chain links but not retroactively reduce any damage dealt by those attacks. Additionally, this effect will not remove any effects that reduce {p} or set the value of {p}. When determining the {p} of attacks that are no longer on the combat chain, last known information (LKI) is used to determine the power right before it ceased to exist and will not be changed by this effect.
Tigerine Reflex’s second ability that can only be activated from your hand. To activate this, show Tigerine Reflex to all players, then pay the cost of discarding it.
Discarding this card to pay the cost of activating its second ability counts as activating an attack reaction ability, not as playing an attack reaction.
You can only activate this attack reaction if you are attacking. You cannot activate it if you are defending.
If the targeted Ninja attack no longer exists when Tigerine Reflex’s activated ability resolves on the stack, you will still create a Crouching Tiger in your hand.

When Tome of Pandemonium resolves, you will banish the top card of each hero’s deck, beginning with you and moving clockwise around the table. You may play each card banished this way this turn. You must still pay for all required costs (e.g. resources points, action points, etc.).

When Topsy Turvy’s instant activated ability resolves, it generates a replacement effect for the remainder of the turn. If a single event would cause one or more cards to be put on top of a deck (e.g. Opt, effects that search and put cards on top of the deck.), those cards are put on the bottom of the deck instead. For example, if you were instructed to Opt 2, you would look at the top 2 cards of your deck, and normally you can choose any combination of cards to go on the top or bottom. If Topsy Turvy’s effect was active, you would put both cards on the bottom of the deck instead in the order of your choosing.

Effects that simply let you look at the top of your deck (e.g. Dash I/O) will not be replaced by Topsy Turvy’s effect. Looking is different from putting a card on top of a deck: the two effects are distinct from each other.

If an effect instructs you to look at the top card of your deck and then gives you an option to put it on the bottom (e.g. Cut to the Chase, Right Behind You, Hornet’s Sting), the card will remain on the top of the deck if you choose to not put it on the bottom. Looking is different from putting a card on top of a deck: the two effects are distinct from each other.

If an effect tells you to look at the top card of your deck, then has subsequent conditional effects that can result in the card being placed either on the top or bottom of the deck (e.g. Pack Call, Firewall), that card will be put on the bottom of your deck under Topsy Turvy’s replacement effect. Looking is different from putting a card on top of a deck: the two effects are distinct from each other. If you were to normally meet the condition to put the card on top of your deck after looking at it, you would put it on the bottom instead.

If you were instructed to Opt 1 while under the effect of Topsy Turvy, you will look at the card; however, the card you look at will be put on the bottom of your deck as you will not be able to put it on the top as part of the Opt effect.

If an effect instructs you to put a card X cards from the top of your deck (e.g. Evo Steel Soul Controller, Submerge), Topsy Turvy’s effect will not modify it. The card will still go in the prescribed position.

If an additional cost requires you to put a card on the top of your deck (e.g. Seek Horizon), you will put the card on the bottom of your deck instead to fulfill that cost. Doing so will meet the condition of paying the additional cost for the purposes of cards and effects.

If an alternative cost allows you to put a card on the top of your deck instead of paying that card’s {r} cost (e.g. Moon Wish, Sun Kiss), you can still use that card’s alternative cost; however, you will instead put it on the bottom of your deck to fulfill that cost. Doing so will meet the condition of paying the additional cost for the purposes of cards and effects.

When activating Touch of Reality, part of its activation cost has a resource cost of X, which you must declare any number for X greater than or equal to 0 that you intend to pay as part of paying its resource costs. Effects that increase resource costs are applied before effects that decrease resource costs; however, this effects will not affect the declared value of X for the purposes of Ward X.When Touch of Reality’s activated ability resolves, it will get Ward X. It will also generate a delayed-triggered effect that will destroy Touch of Reality at the beginning of the end phase.

You can declare the value of X = 0 when activating Touch of Reality. If you do, Touch of Reality will get Ward 0. If you would be dealt damage and you would apply a Ward 0 prevention effect to that damage event, you would still destroy the object with the Ward 0 ability and the damage value of the event is not reduced.
Neither player receives priority during the end phase of a turn.
If Touch of Reality was added as a defending equipment card, you can still activate its instant ability and use its ward ability that it gets.
Tough as a Rok’s ability is functional at all times, regardless of the visibility or zone.
If you attack/defend with this, and then you have less {h} than each other hero, then its power will still be set to 6.

Trench of Watery Depth’s first ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you choose to pitch any blue card from your graveyard when the triggered effect resolves.
You cannot pitch a blue card that is face-down in your graveyard due to this effect.

If you were to hit twice on the same chain link (e.g. hit with a dagger using a “Flick Knives” effect and hit with an attack during the damage step), you would be able to activate Two Step Forward’s activated ability.

Two-Faced’s first ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. When this triggered effect resolves, the attacking hero will draw a card, then you will look at their hand and choose a card. Then that hero will discard the chosen card.
When resolving Two-Faced’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. drawing a card, looking, choosing, discarding).

When Two-Faced triggers and becomes a defending card against an attacking ally, the hero who controls the attacking ally is established as the attacking hero. This is a rule change beginning with Compendium of Rathe.

Unyielding Grip’s first ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you have cards in your hand when you defend with this then have no cards in your hand, this will get +3{d}. The reverse is also true: if you have no cards in your hand when you defend with this then add a card to your hand (e.g. draw), you will no longer have the defense increase.

Valahai Riven’s ability triggers when it becomes a defending card on the combat chain. This is an optional effect: you may pay up to {r}{r}{r}. If you do, you will create a total number of Seismic Surge token equal to the number of {r} paid this way. If you do not pay any resources this way, you do not create any tokens.
When resolving Valahai Riven’s triggered effect, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. paying resources, creating a Seismic Surge token).

When Verdant Tide resolves, it will generate a continuous replacement effect that is active for the remainder of the turn. If you would create 1 or more Elemental or Runeblade aura tokens, you will instead create that many plus 1 of each of those tokens. Additionally, if the Earth Bond ability is active because an Earth card was pitched to play Verdant Tide, you will create an Embodiment of Earth token (which will result in 2 Embodiment of Earth tokens as you will apply the previously generated replacement effect to it).

If you would create two or more different Elemental or Runeblade aura tokens, you create one more of each of those different auras. For example, if a card would have you create 1 runechant and one Embodiment of Earth tokens, you would create 2 of each of those..
If an ability would create 0 Elemental or Runeblade aura tokens, it’s the same as no effect occurring, and therefore the replacement effect generated from Verdant Tide does not apply to it.

If there are multiple replacement effects that can be applied to an event that would create 1 or more aura tokens (e.g. Verdant Tide and Ripple Away), the turn player will decide which player’s effects will be applied first. If a replacement effect is applied first that results in the number of aura tokens created to be 0 (e.g. applying Ripple Away), Verdant Tide’s effect would no longer apply and no aura tokens would be created.

You must pitch at least one Earth card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the Earth Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Earth cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch an Earth card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Earth card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu)

Vestige of Flagellation’s first ability generates a replacement effect that applies to the first time an opponent would gain {h} each turn. This is not optional: the first time an opponent would gain {h} each turn, instead you will lose that much {h} and you will create that many Vigor tokens.
There is no priority in between the effects generated from apply the replacement effect (e.g. losing {h}, creating Vigor tokens)
In a multiplayer game, this replacement effect will apply to the first time any opponent among all opponents would gain {h} each turn.

If an opponent’s first life gain effect is replaced (e.g. Poison the Well) and then has another life gain effect later, Vestige of Flagellation can apply to that new life gain event because it would’ve been the first time they actually gain life and can be replaced.

Volcanic Vice’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you’ve created a Seismic Surge at any point during the turn, it will get spellvoid 3 for the remainder of that turn.

When Voltic Vanguard’s activated ability resolves, it generates a shielding-prevention effect that prevents the next 2 damage that would be dealt to your hero. This can be over separate events of damage (i.e. 1 damage, then 1 damage).

When Voltic Veil resolves, it generates a shielding-prevention effect that prevents the next 4 damage that would be dealt to your hero. This can be over separate events of damage (i.e. 1 arcane damage, then 2 damage, then 1 damage).

You must pitch at least one Lightning card while paying for this card’s resource cost to meet the  Bond condition. Using resources from previously pitched Ice cards (i.e. pitched before playing this card) does not meet this condition.
If you pitch a Lightning card to play this, the condition has been met even if the Lightning card you pitched is removed from the pitch zone (e.g. Potion of Deja Vu).

Walk in My Shoes’ first ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If Walk in My Shoes has {p} less than or equal to its base {p} and then later it has {p} greater than its base, this will get +1{p}.

Walk in My Shoes’ effect is not self-sustaining. If it has power greater than its base from an effect (e.g. +3{p}), then another effect reduces its power by the same amount (e.g. -3{p}), then Walk in My Shoes will lose the +1{p} from its own effect.
Walk in My Shoes’ abilities are not functional while defending.

If Walk in My Shoes’ {p} is less than or equal to its base {p}, then later it has {p} greater than its base during the damage step and causes the if-conditional effect to become active, No Tall Tales gets the +1{p}, but the damage will not be recalculated.

Walk in My Shoes’ second ability is a Crush ability that triggers when Walk in My Shoes deals 4 or more damage to a hero in a single damage event. When the triggered effect resolves, it generates a continuous effect that results in the base {p} and {d} of attack action cards that hero controls to be halved and rounded up until the end of that hero’s next turn.

You choose the modes and targets when you play this card. When resolving Wax and Wane, you will resolve the chosen modes in the order printed on the card. Then, if you had chosen both modes, you will search your deck for a card named Inner Chi, reveal it, shuffle, then put it on top of your deck.
You may not choose the same mode more than once.

You must choose at least one of the modes or both modes. If you choose both modes, you must declare a target for each of the modes chosen as part of playing Wax and Wane. If you control a blue aura with ward, that object can be targeted for both modal abilities.

If you choose both modes and all of the targets for one of the modes no longer exist, you will continue to any remaining effects and modes if able. If the targets for all modes no longer exist, you will still search your deck for an Inner Chi, reveal it, shuffle, and put it on top of your deck.

When searching your deck for an Inner Chi, you can fail to find an Inner Chi even if you still have one in your deck. Nothing is revealed nor put on top of your deck; however, you will still shuffle your deck afterwards.
When resolving Wax and Wane, there is no priority between any of the effects generated (e.g. putting counters, searching, revealing, shuffling, putting it on top of your deck)

When playing Weeping Battleground, its ability is an optional targeted effect. You may choose to not target a hero and doing so means none of the effects banishing an aura or dealing damage are generated. On the other hand, if you wish to use the optional effect, you must target a hero when playing Weeping Battleground; however, you choose whether or not to banish an aura from your graveyard when Weeping Battleground resolves. If you banish an aura from your graveyard this way, you will deal 1 arcane damage to that targeted hero. Then Weeping Battleground is added as a defending card on the combat chain.
When resolving Weeping Battleground, there is no priority between any of the effects generated by the card (e.g. banishing an aura card, dealing arcane damage).
When Whispering Mist resolves, it generates a continuous effect that gives blue attacks and attacks with ephemeral get +1{p} for the remainder of the turn.
This does not affect blue attack action cards that are defending on the combat chain or attacks with ephemeral defending on the combat chain.

When Wind Cutter’s attack reaction ability resolves, you will search your deck for a card with the “shuriken” subtype and put it into the arena. Regardless of if you put a shuriken card into the arena or not, you will shuffle your deck.
When searching your deck for a shuriken card, you can fail to find a shuriken card even if you still have one in your deck. You will still shuffle your deck afterwards.

mBrio Base Cortex’s ability generates a conditional continuous effect that is always checking. If you control a Hyper Driver, it will get +2{d}. If you control a Hyper Driver when this defends, then you lose it, this will lose the +2{d}.
You do not control Hyper Drivers that are sub-cards under objects you control in the arena.

If an aura card does not have a cost, it can not be chosen this way. For example, if you control a Frost Hex that is owned by the opponent and hit them for 3 damage, you may choose to destroy any aura they own that has a cost property of 3 or less which includes the Frost Hex.

Hell Hammer is considered to have attacked if its attack ability is resolved on the stack (and you went from the layer step to the attack step of combat). Targeting objects with Spectra, and closing the combat chain this way, means you are not considered to have attacked with this.
This is conditional on the total number of cards the attacking hero has drawn this turn, not if they’ve drawn 2 or more cards at once this turn.

The triggered ability is given to your next sword attack only, not the sword itself. If you attack with the same sword again in the same turn, it would not have the triggered ability.

This also applies to effects on the card that state that it can be played from the banished zone or trigger while in the banished zone – these are also not functional while a card is face-down.
This is a single card with two faces. You may only have one of these cards in your card-pool.

Once you’ve transformed into either Levia, Redeemed or Blasmophet, Levia Consumed, this card is no longer in your inventory, so you can not transform into its other side.

When you transform your hero into either demi-hero, your old hero goes under your new demi-hero and is considered to be in your soul. The new demi-hero becomes your hero for the remainder of the game. If your old hero is banished from your soul, you do not lose the game.

When you transform into either demi-hero, your life total is reset to the specified base life. This is not considered gaining or losing life for the purposes of rules or effects (e.g. Poison the Well).

When you transform into either demi-hero, effects that specifically applied to your previous hero do not transfer to your demi-hero. E.g. Dishonor’s “rest of the game” effect would not transfer onto your demi-hero.
When a demi-hero is your hero, it is still considered to be a hero for the purposes of rules and effects. E.g. Deal 2 damage to target hero, hero loses all abilities, etc.

If a card is face-down in the banished zone, its abilities are not functional, unless they explicitly state they work while face-down in the banished zone. This also applies to effects on the card that state that it can be played from the banished zone or trigger while in the banished zone (and don’t mention face-down) – these are also not functional while a card is face-down.

Blasmophet’s transformation is optional and triggers when your life total is reduced to exactly 13 from a blood debt triggered effect. You may choose not to transform in Blasmophet when your life total is reduced to 13 this way.

Blood debt triggers resolve one at a time. If you are at 14 life total, with 2 blood debt triggers on the stack, the first will resolve, reducing you to 13, which will then trigger Blasmophet. If you decide to transform, the second blood debt trigger resolves and you would banish a card instead of taking damage. If you decide not to transform, the second blood debt trigger resolves and you lose 1 life point.
Blasmophet’s transformation only triggers when your life is reduced to exactly 13 life total. It does not trigger if blood debt would reduce your hero’s life total to any other number.
Blasmophet’s transformation can trigger multiple times per game, if you increase your life total to above 13 again and let blood debt reduce it to exactly 13.
If you are Blasmophet and you play a card with blood debt from your banished zone using an effect other than Blasmophet’s, you do not consume the once per turn effect of Blasmophet.

If you are Blasmophet, you may play a card with blood debt from your banished zone on your opponent’s turn using Blasmophet’s effect, provided that it is otherwise legal to play that card (e.g. Guardian of the Shadow Realm,, etc.)

If you are Blasmophet and you banish a card (either from an effect or replacing blood debt), you banish it face-up first, then as the triggered effect resolves, turn it face-down. E.g. If you banish a 6 power card face-up, it counts towards effects that are conditional on banishing a 6+ power card, even if it is turned face-down after the fact.

If you are Blasmophet and a card is banished face-down, then Blasmophet’s ability will trigger but the effect that would turn the card face-down will fail. A card banished by intimidate will still return to your hand during the end phase.

This triggered ability resolves after the ability/card has been activated/played. You can not activate Prism, Awakener of Sol/Prism, Advent of Thrones pitching Light of Sol, and then banish the card you put into your soul this way to pay for the rest of the cost of activating.

You may target yourself. If you do, you may choose to fail to choose an attack action card, even if you have one in your hand.
The card chosen must be revealed. If the card can not be revealed (e.g. Channel the Bleak Expanse is in play), it is not put on the bottom of the deck and they do not create a ponder token.

When you play this, you choose the number of cards you will banish to pay the additional cost first, and then you choose the number of modes based on the cost. If you can not pay the additional cost you declared, you can not play this.
You can not choose the same mode more than once.

Morlock hill must be played and resolved before you would be dealt lethal damage, to be active to prevent it.
Lethal damage means you would be dealt damage equal to, or greater than, your hero’s life total.

If you would be dealt lethal damage, and another prevention effect reduces the damage that would be dealt to less than your hero’s life total, the damage is no longer lethal and this prevention effect is not active to prevent the remaining damage.

If this prevention effect applies to an event of damage, the value of the prevention effect is exactly the amount of damage coming in. If an effect would reduce the amount of the prevention effect of Morlock Hill it reduces that value. E.g. If you are at 10 life and would be dealt 10 damage, the prevention effect of Morlock Hill is set to 10. If your opponent has activated Vambrace of Determination, the damage prevention of Morlock Hill would be set to 9 and you would take 1 damage.
The prevention effect is optional. You may choose not to banish Minerva Themis, to prevent lethal damage.
You must pay the action point cost to attack with this.
If you attack with an ally, your hero isn’t considered an attacking hero, and you can not play/activate attack reactions during the reaction step of combat.

If this ally’s life total is reduced to 0, it dies and is removed from the arena.

Hero’s gain life through effects that specify that a hero gains life. Changing your hero into another hero (e.g. Levia, Redeemed) is not considered gaining life, even if the life total for that player increases.

If you play and resolve a card with an arcane damage effect before activating Surgent Aethertide, it will not retroactively apply the effect and will not apply to the next card you play with an arcane damage effect.
The card with the arcane damage effect does not have to target the same opposing hero that Surgent Aethertide dealt damage to in order to gain the effect.

To banish an opponent’s cards, you must be the one instructed by an effect to do the banishing. If an opponent is instructed by an effect to banish a card, it does not complete the contract because YOU are not banishing an opponent’s card.
This contract can be completed any number of times, as long as this is face-up in the arena.
Banishing multiple blue cards at the same time completes this contract that many times. I.e. the contract is completed with each blue card banished.
You must deal more than that much damage (after prevention effects have been applied) for the surge condition to be met.
The surge effect is not a triggered effect. There is no priority between dealing damage and the surge effect resolving.
Activating Tearing Shuko’s ability does not close the combat chain.
If you play a card that does not have the specified cost, or does not have an arcane damage effect, it does not consume the effect.

Cards with costs that include X, are considered to have the cost determined by the player that plays it. E.g. If the card has cost X, and player declares X=2 when playing the card, the card is considered to have cost 2 for the purposes of this effect.
You only control cards that are in the arena (e.g. on the combat chain). Crouching Tigers in your banished zone are not considered under your control.
Crouching Tiger is a card, and when it is created this way, it does not count towards the card limit for the format.
If you do not play Crouching Tiger the turn it was created by this card’s effect, it remains in the banished zone.
Tome of Aeo triggers at the beginning of your action phase. Players can play cards or activate abilities before Tome of Aeo’s trigger-layer resolves off the stack.
A card or token with a triggered effect that has a triggering condition when it enters the arena may trigger when it returns to the arena after Tranquil Passing leaves the arena.
A card banished this way returns to the arena immediately after Tranquil Passing leaves the arena. There is no priority between the two events.
If you banish a token with Tranquil Passing, the token ceases to exist and does not return when Tranquil Passing leaves the arena.
You may banish any target aura token (token’s typically don’t have a cost), but can only banish non-token aura tokens with the specified cost.

You can only target another hero – you cannot target yourself.

If a token Aura is destroyed by Crown of Reflection, you may put an Illusionist aura from your hand into the arena with a cost of 0. In this way tokens are assumed to have a cost of 0 because the effect assumes and requires the cost to be defined, instead being conditional on it.

If the aura destroyed has a triggered effect that triggers on its own destruction (e.g. Haze Bending), that effect will trigger. If the illusionist aura you put into the arena has a triggered effect that triggers on other aura’s destruction, that effect will not trigger because it was not in the arena at the time the previous aura was destroyed.
If the Aura is destroyed before the activated layer resolves as a layer on the stack, the layer fails to resolve and you do not gain the benefit of putting an aura into the arena.
You cannot defend with Dissolution Sphere as it has no defense value. (This is different than if the defense value was 0.)
If you would take 2 or more damage from an event, Dissolution Sphere would not prevent that damage and you would take the 2 damage. Dissolution sphere only prevents events that deal 1 damage.
If you would take mixed damage from an event, and the total damage is greater than 2, Dissolution Sphere’s prevention effect does not apply.
If you would take 2 or more damage from an event and another replacement effect reduces the damage to be dealt to 1, the Dissolution Sphere’s prevention effect does apply and prevents 1 damage.

Physical damage ({p} damage) is any damage done by an attack such as a weapon attack or attack action during the damage step of combat. Loss of life, arcane damage and untyped damage do not count as {p} damage.
The “damage dealt this turn” is the total damage of all types dealt this turn including arcane damage, {p} damage, and untyped damage, but does not include loss of life effects.

The controller of Fractal Replication gets to choose the order of its effects when they are triggered. E.g If Fractal Replication gains multiple effects that state ‘when this hits…’ the controlling player gets to choose the order in which these effects resolve.

Fractal Replciation’s triggered effect is dependent on the Illusionist type. It is applied after types of objects have been determined, and gains the base abilities of an Illusionist attack action cards.
Defending cards retain their abilities, but they are not considered functional. Therefore Fractal Replication still gains any defending Illusionist attack action card’s base abilities.

Illusionist attack action cards that are no longer on the combat chain (such as when they are put into Soul, or back into the deck) are not considered for Fractal Replciation’s effects. Last Known Information is not applied in this case.

Each player is affected in clockwise-order starting from the turn player. Once a player has put a card from their hand on the bottom of their deck (and drawn a card), or not, the next player in clockwise-order makes their decision.

Opposing players only draw from the effect if they decide to put a card from their hand on the bottom of their deck. The effect is optional and players can decide not to put a card from their hand on the bottom of their deck.
